Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: NWB days 0-14; WBAT in protective device at post-op wk 2; full D/C boot and heel lifts by wk 8
Key Restrictions: No aggressive stretching of Achilles or gastroc-soleus complex before 12 wks; risk factors: age 30-50, male, fluoroquinolone use
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases; recovery 6-9 months; return to sport 9-12 months
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; return to run criteria: 25 SL heel raises within 20% of uninvolved, 95% symmetry ROM and calf circumference; return to sport: <10% PF asymmetry, 90% Y-balance, 90% hop testing; LEFS/FAAM/ATRS outcome measures; extensive references

WB Status: NWB on crutches wks 0-2; WBAT using crutches wks 2-4; wean crutches wks 4-6; FWB wk 6+
Key Restrictions: Plaster cast/rigid boot with foot in 20 deg PF and 3 wedges (22/16/10 mm); remove wedges progressively; wear boot 24 hrs/day; no running until 12 wks; no plyometrics until 16 wks
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Phase I REST (0-2 wks), Phase II Easy Walking (2-4 wks), Phase III Active Recovery (4-6 wks), Phase IV Strengthening (6-10 wks), Phase V Sport Prep (10-16 wks), Phase VI Return to Sport (16+ wks)
Notes: .edu site; non-operative protocol; wedge system for progressive DF; gentle supervised PF exercises at wk 2; return to sport criteria: full ROM, 90% strength, sport-specific drills completed

WB Status: NWB in splint/boot 0-3 wks; PWB wk 4 (progress 25% BW/wk); FWB by wk 7 in boot; boot D/C wk 8
Key Restrictions: Avoid over-elongation of Achilles; conservative approach for augmentation, re-rupture, revision, chronic tendinosis, obesity, older age, steroid use; wedge weaning: 3 wedges wk 4, 2 wk 5, 1 wk 6, 0 wk 7; no aggressive eccentric loading before 12 wks
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-3 wks), Phase II (4-6 wks), Phase III (7-9 wks), Phase IV (10-14 wks), Phase V (return to sport); includes return-to-run and agility/plyo programs
Notes: Criterion-based; DF to neutral by wk 6; heel raise endurance test; functional assessment >80% for return to sport; references included

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopedic Surgery, Foot & Ankle / Sports Medicine
WB Status: NWB in splint wks 0-2; TDWB in boot wks 2-4; WBAT in boot wks 5-6; FWB wk 7+; boot D/C wk 8
Key Restrictions: No ankle/foot ROM wks 0-2; PROM limited to DF 0 deg with knee flexed to 90 wks 2-4; no passive Achilles stretching wks 2-4; no resisted eversion wks 2-4; avoid aggressive eccentric loading; splint in 20 deg PF
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I Recovery (0-6 wks), Phase II Rehabilitation (7-12 wks), Phase III Restoration (13-16 wks), Phase IV Return to Sport (17+ wks)
Notes: .edu site; heel raise progression: bilateral seated → bilateral standing → unilateral; running criteria: 3 sets of 15 unilateral heel raises with BW; shuttle/Pilates for sub-max accel/decel before full plyometrics

WB Status: NWB until allowed by physician; progressive WB in boot (25% increments every 1-2 wks); scale recommended to estimate WB
Key Restrictions: Cast or boot to immobilize; Weber/AO and Lauge-Hansen classification systems explained; wean boot to air cast or ASO in normal shoe; PT starts 2-6 wks post-injury focusing on motion/swelling first
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I Initial (NWB, cast/boot), Phase II Early Motion (begin ROM/WB per MD), Phase III Progressive Loading (closed chain, dynamic balance), Phase IV Return to Activity
Notes: Includes detailed classification systems; pre-injury gait/balance/crutch training recommended; return to sport: SL heel rise or SL hop on injured side; progressive balance (mini tramp, BAPS, Star excursion)

WB Status: NWB initially; progressive WB per surgeon; varies by fracture stability and pattern
Key Restrictions: Cast or boot immobilization; early AROM once wounds healed (~2 wks); edema control and scar massage at 2 wks; WB usually allowed 4-6 wks; PROM at 6 wks; driving: 8 wks if right ankle (automatic only for left)
Timeline / Phases: Time-based progression dependent on fracture pattern and healing; surgeon determines WB timing via X-ray
Notes: Return to sport criteria: single heel rise on injured side OR single leg hop; may take 6 months to 1 year; includes both operative and non-operative pathways in same document

WB Status: FWB as tolerated in walking boot; D/C crutches/AD as quickly as possible; boot x 4 wks then transition to regular shoes with ankle brace
Key Restrictions: Boot is protective not immobilizing — remove when seated/lying; alphabet exercises 2x/day; towel Achilles stretch 5x hold 5 sec; ankle brace during transition to shoes; step-wise activity ramp-up
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Boot phase (0-4 wks), Transition to shoes (4-6 wks), Progressive activity (6+ wks); 10-12 wks for full healing
Notes: Web-based protocol (not PDF); notably more aggressive early WB approach (FWB day 1 in boot); patient-friendly language; emphasizes boot removal for exercises

WB Status: WB status per surgeon; boot/cast per surgeon; typically NWB initially progressing per surgeon clearance
Key Restrictions: No joint mobs near fracture site or stabilizing over fracture; no IASTM over fracture sites until 6 wks; avoid pressure on healing fracture sites/hardware; pilon fractures have more guarded prognosis; close communication with surgeon critical
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks), Phase II (7-12 wks), Phase III (13-16 wks), Phase IV (return to sport); includes return-to-run and agility/plyo programs
Notes: Criterion-based; foot joint mobs OK if away from fracture site; scar mob once incisions healed; joint position sense <5 deg error for Phase III; functional assessment >80% for return to sport; references included

WB Status: WB status per surgeon; boot/cast per surgeon
Key Restrictions: No joint mobs near fracture site; no IASTM over fracture sites until 6 wks; avoid pressure on healing sites/hardware; simpler fractures progress faster; close collaboration with surgeon for timing
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks), Phase II (7-12 wks), Phase III (13-16 wks), Phase IV (return to sport)
Notes: Adopted from MGH Sports Medicine framework; pain <3/10 and minimal swelling to progress; volumetric or Figure 8 swelling measures; return-to-run and agility/plyo programs included

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopedic Surgery, Foot & Ankle
WB Status: NWB 0-3 wks; WB advancement per MD/fracture type wks 3-6; WB status and restrictions per MD wks 6-12; no restrictions if healing complete wks 12+
Key Restrictions: Boot/cast per MD; no impact or torsional exercises/activities wks 6-12; WB advancement and activity restrictions per MD and fracture type; pilon fractures have more conservative care plan; short leg splint/cast/boot x 6-12 wks per surgeon
Timeline / Phases: 7 phases: Pre-op, Post-op (0-3 wks), 3-6 wks, 6-12 wks, 12-16 wks, 16-20 wks, 4-6 months; low impact sports at 16-20 wks; high impact sports at 4-6 months
Notes: .edu site; includes pre-op phase (gait assessment, AD instruction NWB); joint mobilization at 6-12 wks to restore foot/ankle motion; SL activities on varying surfaces at 12-16 wks; low impact return (cycling, rowing, swimming, Stairmaster, elliptical) at 16-20 wks; high impact return (jogging, running, jumping, multi-planar movements, uneven outdoor surfaces) at 4-6 months

Source Organization: Dr. Kevin Roth MD (Palo Alto Medical Foundation)
WB Status: NWB wks 0-6; WBAT wk 6+ (progress over 2-4 wks)
Key Restrictions: Splint on at all times wks 0-2; transition to CAM boot wks 2-6; wean out of boot over 2-4 wks after wk 6; begin active/passive ankle ROM (DF/PF) wks 2-6; air alphabet; continue knee/hip ROM
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks), Phase II (2-6 wks), Phase III (6+ wks with full AROM/PROM, gait/balance, proprioception)
Notes: Concise single-page format; traditional NWB x 6 wks approach; good for quick reference

WB Status: WBAT in CAM boot Day 1; crutches for ambulation; advance WB as tolerated
Key Restrictions: CAM boot for stabilization and wound protection; sleep in boot; hang operative extremity down 1 min every hour for circulation then re-elevate; gentle ankle ROM day 10-14 (avoid inversion/eversion initially); sutures removed when wound healed
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I wound healing/edema control (Day 1-14), Phase II ROM/strengthening, Phase III return to activity
Notes: Advanced/early WB protocol; notably more aggressive than traditional NWB x 6 wks approach; fracture type and fixation may dictate alterations

WB Status: Strict NWB x 6 wks after surgery; advance WB over 4 wks
Key Restrictions: Complete immobilization in boot/cast wks 1-6; boot for out of bed wks 7-10; boot for work/school wks 11-12; brace until stable proprioception with eyes closed; brace for at-risk activities x 1 year; beware increased swelling/pain = incomplete healing; diabetes patients delay 2-4 wks and progress slowly
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Immobilization (0-6 wks), Early Rehab (7-10 wks), Progressive Loading (11-14 wks), Return to Activity (14+ wks); PT begins after 6 wks immobilization
Notes: Covers both ankle and foot fractures; bones heal ~70% strength at 7 wks; conservative approach; diabetes modification noted; 1-year brace recommendation for at-risk activities

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: FWB; no acute WB restrictions; bracing/taping for functional activities
Key Restrictions: CAI defined as instability persisting >6 months post-sprain; no acute injury restrictions; no inversion or eversion restrictions; talocrural/hindfoot/midfoot mobilizations; peroneal neuropathy screening (repeated sprains may propagate); Beighton Scale for systemic hypermobility; hindfoot/midfoot alignment assessment
Timeline / Phases: 2 phases: Phase I Acute/Return to Activity, Phase II Return to Sport/Discharge; criterion-based throughout; no strict time-based phase durations
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; 2019; covers chronic not acute ankle sprain; return to sport criteria: subjective outcome >90%, DF lunge >7.5 cm, Y-Balance >90% composite, Foot Lift Test <5 errors, hop testing >90% LSI; outcome tools: FAAM, TSK-11, Cumberland Ankle Instability Tool; risk factors table (modifiable and non-modifiable); differential diagnosis: peroneal neuropathy, peroneal tendinopathy, syndesmotic instability, cuboid syndrome, osteochondral lesion

WB Status: WBAT with brace/protective tape for WB activities; immobilization x 10 days for severe sprain
Key Restrictions: Brace or protective tape during all WB activities; Ottawa rule to rule out fracture; Grade I: 1-2 wks; Grade II: 2-4 wks; Grade III: up to 9-10 wks; avoid aggressive inversion strengthening early; no forced inversion ROM
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I Protection/Optimal Loading (1-2 wks), Phase II Intermediate/Sub-Acute (3-6 wks), Phase III Late/Chronic (7-10 wks), Phase IV Return to Sport/Function (11-16 wks)
Notes: Criterion-based; Ottawa ankle rule noted; Grades I-IV manual therapy for pain; differential diagnosis listed (fracture, syndesmosis, osteochondral, peroneal/Achilles tendon strain); return to sport criteria: 90% Y-Balance/Star Balance vs uninvolved, full ankle strength; Cumberland Ankle Instability Tool or FAAM for outcomes; SL heel raise test (25 reps)

WB Status: WBAT; crutches PRN; Grade I: crutches PRN + taping; Grade II: crutches until normal gait + ASO brace; Grade III-IV: crutches + CAM boot x 4-6 wks
Key Restrictions: Grade I-IV sprain classification table with treatment and return-to-activity timelines; Grade IV (high ankle): moderate-severe pain, mild swelling, crutches + CAM x 4-6 wks, return 12-16 wks; avoid increasing ankle ligament laxity during early ROM; surgical intervention required in 15-20% when conservative management fails
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I Protect/Healing (0-4 wks), Phase II Intermediate (4-8 wks), Phase III Late/Functional (8+ wks)
Notes: Includes high ankle (syndesmotic) sprain classification and treatment; Y-Balance, SL hop, triple hop, crossover hop for functional testing; return to sport determined by MD when full ROM, normal strength, no pain, sport-specific activities achieved

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopedic Surgery, Foot & Ankle
WB Status: NWB on surgical foot until at least 12 wks post-op; progressive WB starting at 12 wks (start 20 lbs, increase 20 lbs every 2-3 days over 1 month); joint depression fracture: hold at 40 lbs x 1 month before progressing; FWB with good gait by wks 16-17
Key Restrictions: Leg elevated 8-10 inches from bed-level; bulky Jones splint post-op; foot in neutral at rest; limit time with extremity dependent throughout; swimming OK at 6 wks but no walking in water and no aggressive kicking; NO dead lift/clean and jerk weight lifting after 6 months; if increased pain/swelling: back off WB and rebuild gradually; joint depression fracture type is more conservative
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Pre-op, Post-op (0-2 wks), 2-6 wks, 6-12 wks, 12 wks (WB begins), 4-6 months; pre-op PT visit required for red flag screening (bilateral, prior failure, age >65, UE injury, comorbidities)
Notes: .edu site; includes mandatory pre-op PT assessment; red flag screening criteria listed; POD 1 toe and intrinsic ROM; STJ A/PROM begins at 2-3 wk visit; grade 1-2 joint mobs at 6-7 wks; OTC orthotics (Spenco/Superfeet) at 12 wks; balance and proprioception when FWB; functional assessment at 6 months (timed SL stance, heel raise, squats, step-ups)

Source Organization: Journal of Foot and Ankle Surgery (Asia Pacific)
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): B. Adhya PT; H. Dhillon PT; S. Dhillon MD; M.S. Dhillon MD — PGIMER Chandigarh & Flinders University
WB Status: NWB in cast/brace wks 1-4; PWB with crutches/walker wks 5-8 (guided by radiology and surgeon); FWB initiated wks 9-12; progressive WB before 8 wks not recommended to minimize late articular collapse
Key Restrictions: Cast with ankle in neutral (sometimes slight eversion); elevation wks 1-4; no sitting with limb gravity-dependent for prolonged periods; AROM exercises hourly (tibiotalar, subtalar, midtarsal, toe joints) starting hrs 24-72; toe flexion important to prevent contractures; back slab orthosis to prevent equinus contracture; special precautions for bilateral fractures (wheelchair mobility, log roll transfers, TLS brace if concurrent spinal fracture)
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Weeks 1-4 (immobilization/pain/edema), Weeks 5-8 (progressive WB + vigorous isometrics/ROM), Weeks 9-12 (FWB + gait normalization + strengthening); includes calcaneal stress fracture sub-protocol (NWB-PWB x 6 wks then graded running; return to sport 6-8 wks)
Notes: Peer-reviewed journal article (Open Access CC BY 4.0); covers non-operative, post-operative, stress fracture, bilateral calcaneal, and bilateral calcaneal + spinal fracture protocols; pool exercises at wk 3 (non-op) and wk 3 (op); plyometrics only after complete bone healing; 65% limited in vigorous/sport activities long-term; special bilateral/spinal fracture table; 18 references; MIS approach: ROM within 3 days; extensile incision: ROM at 10-12 days

WB Status: NWB x 6 wks in splint/boot; progressive WB beginning wk 6 with orthotics; full activity 6-18 months
Key Restrictions: Elevation above heart 23 hrs/day wks 0-2; ice behind knee 30 min on/off; DO NOT attempt subtalar (side-to-side) motion until 6-8 wks and fracture healed per MD; focus DF/PF only in early ROM; no WB before wk 6; driving: wk 9 if right ankle surgery, when off narcotics for left ankle (automatic only)
Timeline / Phases: 2 phases: Phase I Protection (0-6 wks with 0-2, 3-4, 4-6 wk sub-phases), Phase II WB and Early Strengthening (6-12 wks); PT starts 4-6 wks post-injury
Notes: Non-operative indicated for Type I (non-displaced) and some Type II fractures (up to 2 mm displacement); also used when poor soft tissue, age, tobacco use, or comorbidities preclude surgery; subtalar joint mobilization explicitly withheld until 6-8 wks and MD clearance; orthotics at 6-10 wks; proprioception (SLS, mini-tramp, rocker board, dynadisc) Phase II; outcomes same as operative entry: 65% limited in vigorous/sport, 50% ambulate any surface, 40% unable to return to prior employment

Surgery Type: Calcaneus Fracture — Operative (ORIF or Subtalar Fusion)
WB Status: NWB x 8 wks in splint/cast/boot; progressive WB in boot starting wk 8-12 (25% increments every 1-2 wks per X-ray confirmation of healing); transition to shoe wks 12-14; FWB wks 16-20
Key Restrictions: Strict elevation above heart 23 hrs/day wks 0-2; ice behind knee 30 min on/off; NWB x 8 wks regardless of pain; WB only when X-rays demonstrate adequate healing; subtalar arthrodesis: mobs to TC/mid/forefoot only (not fused joint); ORIF: subtalar mobs OK; no joint mobs to fused joints; scale recommended for estimating WB percentage; orthotics in final phase
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I Protection (0-6 wks with 0-2, 2-3, 4-5 wk sub-phases), Phase II ROM/Early Strengthening (6-12 wks), Phase III Progressive Strengthening (12-20 wks); pre-op PT phase for gait/crutch/knee scooter training
Notes: Includes pre-op PT; detailed fracture classification (Type I-IV); covers both ORIF and subtalar fusion with separate ROM/mob guidelines per procedure; complications noted (arthritis, peroneal impingement, sural nerve injury); outcomes: 65% limited in vigorous/sport, 50% ambulate any surface, 40% unable to return to prior employment; stationary bike in boot at 6 wks; use scale to estimate WB %

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: NWB in CAM boot wks 0-2; progressive WB in boot wks 2-6; FWB wk 6+
Key Restrictions: No active or passive ankle inversion or eversion x 6 wks; low grade joint mobs of accessory joints only; 90-95% excellent outcomes reported; Brostrom-Gould variation (ATFL repair)
Timeline / Phases: 3 criterion-based phases: Acute Phase (0-6 wks), Return to Function (6-12 wks), Return to Sport (12-26 wks)
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; 2022 update; SL stance test (foot lifts in 30 sec); return to sport criteria: normalized gait, full pain-free ROM, SL balance 30 sec, sport-specific movements; extensive references

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopedic Surgery, Foot & Ankle / Sports Medicine
WB Status: NWB in splint wks 0-2; WBAT in boot wks 2-4; WBAT in boot all ADLs wks 4-6; WBAT in lace-up ASO wks 6-8
Key Restrictions: NO INVERSION until 12 wks; no ankle/foot ROM wks 0-4; no talar distraction or anterior glides; wean to ASO brace wks 6-8; intrinsic foot strengthening excluding inversion
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I Recovery (0-8 wks with sub-phases 0-4, 4-6, 6-8), Phase II Rehabilitation (8-12 wks), Phase III Restoration (12-16 wks), Phase IV Return to Sport (16+ wks)
Notes: .edu site; strength goals: 75% uninvolved by Phase III, 90% by Phase IV; balance: SL stance 30 sec eyes closed; jump deceleration/eccentric training before advancing to plyometrics

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): MGH Sports Medicine / Foot & Ankle Service
WB Status: NWB on crutches in splint wks 0-2; NWB walking wks 3-5 (may WB in boot standing only for ADLs); WBAT in walking boot wk 6; wean to supportive sneaker wks 7-8
Key Restrictions: Elevation 'toes above nose' critical (max 2-3 hrs below heart first 2 wks); swelling normal up to 6-12 months; keep boot on at night; if tendon transfers: no F/A strengthening against resistance or stretching of involved musculature until 3 months; procedure not expected to increase ROM significantly; NO return to high-impact activity ever
Timeline / Phases: 7 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks), Phase II (3-5 wks), Phase III (6-10 wks), Phase IV (11-13 wks), Phase V (14-16 wks), Phase VI (4-6 months), Phase VII (6+ months return to sport)
Notes: Criterion-based; detailed intrinsic foot exercises in Phase II (arch doming, toe piano, toe spreads); Heel Rise test >90% uninvolved for Phase VII; SL balance >30 sec level surface; I-PRRS psychological readiness scale included; Y-Balance and Star Excursion for return to low-impact sport; MGH explicit recommendation: no return to repetitive high-impact sport/occupation/activity

Surgery Type: ACL Reconstruction with Medial Meniscus Repair
WB Status: TTWB with crutches and LROM brace locked in full ext (0-6 wks); progress after 6 wks
Key Restrictions: LROM brace locked at 0 deg for sleeping and walking; no flex past 90 deg x 6 wks; no pillows under knee; BFR therapy starting 6 days post-op; hamstring autograft: may need to delay HS isometrics
Timeline / Phases: 7 time-based phases: 1-4 days, 4-14 days, 2-6 wks, 6-8 wks, 8-12 wks, 12-16 wks, 16-20+ wks
Notes: Medial meniscus repair-specific; includes pre-op phase with LROM brace fitting; BFR with NMES emphasized; detailed day-by-day early progression; functional knee brace measurement at 12-16 wks; same practice as hip labral repair protocol in database

Surgery Type: ACL Reconstruction with Meniscus Repair or Microfracture
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): D. Diduch MD; M. Miller MD; E. Carson MD; S. Brockmeier MD; F. Gwathmey MD
WB Status: TTWB (25%) x 2 wks; 50% WB wks 2-6; FWB after 6 wks; D/C crutches Phase 2
Key Restrictions: Brace 0-90 deg x 6 wks (unlocked at all times after nerve block resolves); no forced flex x 6 wks; hamstring autograft: delay HS strengthening x 12 wks, heel slides to 90 only; running at 12 wks BPTB, 16 wks hamstring
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase 1 (0-6 wks), Phase 2 (6-10 wks), Phase 3 (10-16 wks), Phase 4 (16+ wks)
Notes: 2023 updated version (replaces earlier UVA protocol already in database); differentiates BPTB vs hamstring throughout; STEP test at 4 months; pool running at 8 wks; isokinetic 70% uninvolved for Phase 4; NMES for quad

WB Status: Per graft type: BPTB WBAT; Hamstring PWB initially; Allograft more conservative; meniscal recon: avoid WB >45 deg flex x 4 wks, limit to 90 deg wks 4-8
Key Restrictions: BPTB: increased anterior knee pain with kneeling; Hamstring: no resisted hamstring x 12 wks; Allograft: slightly increased graft failure risk; locked hinged brace post-op; meniscal recon specific WB/ROM restrictions
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases adopted from Adams et al (2012) JOSPT and Delaware PT Clinic Guidelines; includes hop testing and functional assessment
Notes: Comprehensive protocol covering all graft types; 2015; based on Adams et al JOSPT 2012; hop test criteria: >=80% strength and >=85% functional testing for return to sport; separate from BWH's graft-specific protocols

WB Status: WBAT on crutches with active quad contraction; D/C crutches when normal gait, no SLR lag, effusion <=2+
Key Restrictions: D/C brace/immobilizer when SLR without lag and visible quad activation; allografts 12+ months return to sport; no unnecessary WB throughout day to minimize effusion; concomitant procedure modifications in appendix
Timeline / Phases: Pre-op phase + 5 post-op phases: Immediate (wk 1), Early (wk 2-3), Intermediate (wk 4-8), Late (wk 9-16), Return to Sport (wk 16+); 20-46 total visits expected; return to sport 9-12 months (allografts 12+)
Notes: Comprehensive criterion-based guideline; includes pre-op rehab with QI >=80% goal; NMES dosed at >=50% MVIC; BFR training as option; soreness rules appendix; sweep test for effusion; quad sets 5-10 sets of 100/day; covers modifications for meniscal repair, meniscectomy, microfracture, chondroplasty, MCL, PLC; outcome measures: KOS-ADLS, IKDC, ACL-RSI, Marx

WB Status: PWB with crutches x 6 wks (unless otherwise instructed by MD)
Key Restrictions: Same as BPTB plus: PWB with crutches x 6 wks; early return to sport phase delayed; allograft revascularization is slower than autograft
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: same as BPTB/hamstring entries; early return to sport phase delayed
Notes: Same document as BPTB/hamstring entries; notes allograft has slightly increased risk of graft failure with early high-level activity

WB Status: TDWB x 2 wks; PWB wks 2-4; WBAT wks 4-6; crutches and brace x 6 wks
Key Restrictions: Allograft revascularization slower than autograft; crutches and brace continued x 6 wks; brace locked ext x 1 wk, remove for rehab/sleep after wk 1, worn in vulnerable situations wks 6-12, optional after 12 wks; for meniscus repair avoid OKC hamstring x 6 wks
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks, 1-2 visits/wk), Phase II (6-8 wks, 2-3 visits/wk), Phase III (2-6 months, 2-3 visits/wk), Phase IV-V (6+ months, D/C after functional progression); supervised PT 3-9 months
Notes: Allograft-specific; most conservative WB progression of BWH ACL protocols; Phase I extends to 6 wks (vs 4 wks for autografts)

Source Organization: Dr. Brian Cole MD (Rush University Medical Center)
WB Status: WBAT with crutches (Phase I); gradually D/C crutches (Phase II)
Key Restrictions: Brace locked full ext for ambulation and sleeping x 1 wk; unlocked wks 1-4; D/C brace when full ext and no lag; ROM as tolerated; SLR with brace in full ext until quad prevents lag
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Phase I (0-4 wks), Phase II (4-6 wks), Phase III (6-8 wks), Phase IV (8-12 wks), Phase V (12-16 wks), Phase VI (16-20+ wks)
Notes: Covers both allograft and autograft BPTB; modified with concurrent meniscus repair/transplant or articular cartilage procedure; copyright 2003

WB Status: WBAT initially with brace locked and crutches; may walk without crutches when no increased pain/effusion and proper gait
Key Restrictions: Brace locked initially, unlock once SLR without lag; D/C brace after 6 wks per MD; no towel under knee; no active kicking into extension; no pivoting on surgical side; emphasize patellar mobilizations for BPTB
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks), Phase II (3-5 wks), Phase III (6-8 wks), Phase IV (9-12 wks), Phase V (early return to sport), Phase VI (unrestricted return to sport); includes return-to-run and agility/plyo programs
Notes: Single document covers BPTB, hamstring, and allograft with graft-specific modifications; NMES 2500 Hz 75 bursts; criterion-based; patellar mobs heavily emphasized for BPTB; includes references

WB Status: WBAT immediately with crutches and brace
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in ext for sleep x 1 wk; brace locked for ambulation until full ext with good quad control; avoid hyperextension >10 deg; flex to 90 by wk 4
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I (0-4 wks), Phase II (4-10 wks), Phase III (10+ wks); supervised PT 3-6 months
Notes: BPTB-specific; protect graft revascularization (8 wks) and fixation (4-6 wks); CPM not commonly used; wean crutches/brace by 4 wks; pool running at 8 wks; for meniscus repair defer to meniscus protocol

WB Status: PWB with crutches x 6 wks (unless otherwise instructed by MD)
Key Restrictions: Same as BPTB plus: no resisted hamstring strengthening until 12 wks; PWB with crutches x 6 wks; early return to sport phase delayed
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks), Phase II (3-5 wks), Phase III (6-8 wks), Phase IV (9-12 wks), Phase V (early return to sport), Phase VI (unrestricted return to sport)
Notes: Same document as BPTB entry; hamstring-specific modifications noted throughout; delayed hamstring loading is key differentiator

WB Status: PWB with crutches (0-1 wk); progress to FWB by 4 wks
Key Restrictions: Protect graft revascularization (8 wks) and fixation (8-12 wks); flex to 90 deg only Phase I; avoid hyperext >10 deg; delay hamstring strengthening x 12 wks; very gentle hamstring stretching at 1 wk; brace locked ext x 1 wk, unlock <90 wks 1-3, wean wks 3-4
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I (0-4 wks), Phase II (4-12 wks), Phase III (12+ wks); supervised PT 4-7 months
Notes: Hamstring-specific; longer graft fixation protection (8-12 wks vs 4-6 wks BPTB); CKC quad strengthening delayed to Phase II; for meniscus repair/transplant defer to meniscus protocol

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): C. Brunst PT DPT SCS AT; K. Martin PT DPT SCS; Reviewers: E. Luse PT DPT; S. Di Stasi PT PhD; L. Schmitt PT PhD; R. Magnussen MD
WB Status: Criterion-based AD discharge (same criteria as OSU quad tendon protocol)
Key Restrictions: No isotonic resisted hamstring exercises x 8 wks; no loaded OKC ext beyond 45 deg x 8 wks; no forced flex past 90 for meniscus repairs; meniscus repair: PWB x 4 wks, no WB exercise >90 x 8 wks
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases: Protection/Early Loading, Strengthening, Return to Function (12 wks+); 1-2 visits/wk with HEP emphasis 2-3 days/wk
Notes: Hamstring-specific; 2023 update; MOON group recommendations; no resisted hamstring x 8 wks (vs BWH's 12 wks — clinically notable difference); isokinetic testing no earlier than 12 wks

WB Status: WBAT with crutches; wean as tolerated; no brace needed
Key Restrictions: Hamstring autograft: delay hamstring strengthening x 12 wks, heel slides to 90 deg; BPTB: hamstring curls adding weight as tolerated, CKC quad strengthening OK; knee flex 0-120 as tolerated
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase 1 (0-4 wks), Phase 2 (4-8 wks), Phase 3 (8-16 wks), Phase 4 (16+ wks)
Notes: Differentiates BPTB vs hamstring autograft in strengthening guidelines; no brace needed; no immobilizer after nerve block wears off

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: Criterion-based AD discharge (full active ext, strong quad iso, 2x10 SLR without lag, effusion <=1+, normalized gait)
Key Restrictions: No forced flex past 90 deg for meniscus repairs; ACLR with meniscectomy can push for symmetrical flex; patellar taping/Cho-Pat strap for PF symptoms; no loaded OKC ext beyond 45 deg x 8 wks
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases (not time-based): Protection/Early Loading, Strengthening, Return to Function (12 wks - return to sport); MOON group recommendations
Notes: Quad tendon-specific; 2023 update; isokinetic testing 80%+ at 60/sec and 300/sec; refers to separate OSU protocols for hamstring and BPTB; based on MOON group; includes references to UDel guidelines

WB Status: WBAT with crutches; many patients have pain limiting WB first 2 wks; crutches PRN
Key Restrictions: Avoid dynamic knee valgus and femoral IR throughout — maintenance of neutral LE alignment (ASIS over knee over 2nd toe) stressed; milestone-based (not time-based) progression strongly emphasized; for non-sport patients: formal PT likely ends at 3-4 months but recovery continues many months and must remain diligent with strengthening; bone realignment concomitant: different protocol; signs of infection (swelling, redness, drainage, warmth, fever, chills) or DVT (calf swelling/tenderness/redness) require immediate contact
Timeline / Phases: Milestone-based: WBAT/D/C crutches (wk 2-3), Brace open (wk 2-4), Full ROM (wk 4-6), Jogging (wk 10-12), Sport-specific (3-6 months), Return to sport (4-9 months per surgeon clearance with strength and motion analysis testing)
Notes: Unique: explicitly milestone-based rather than time-based — philosophy described in detail; return to sport testing includes strength AND motion analysis prior to clearance; non-sport patients counseled that recovery continues long after PT ends; bone realignment concomitant patients excluded (different protocol); detailed philosophy on avoiding dynamic valgus and femoral IR

Source Organization: Dr. David P. Trofa MD (Columbia University Medical Center)
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dr. David P. Trofa MD (Head Team Physician, Columbia University)
WB Status: WBAT in brace locked extension; crutches x 2-4 wks; D/C crutches when normal gait
Key Restrictions: Brace locked extension x 2 wks; brace 0-90 wks 2-4; D/C brace wk 6; no lateral patellar mobilizations; no deep squats or lunges until wk 12; no OKC knee extension until wk 8; avoid patellar compression exercises early
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, brace locked ext, WBAT, PROM, quad sets + NMES, SLR), Phase II (2-6 wks, brace 0-90, progressive ROM/strengthening, bike wk 4), Phase III (6-12 wks, D/C brace, progressive CKC/OKC, begin jogging criteria-based), Phase IV (12+ wks, sport-specific, plyometrics, return to sport)
Notes: Columbia University Medical Center; Head Team Physician Columbia University; concise 4-phase format; NMES for quad activation; return to sport after physician clearance

WB Status: Brace locked, PWB wks 0-1; WBAT with crutches per MD; walk without crutches when no increased pain/effusion/proper gait
Key Restrictions: Keep knee straight and elevated when sitting/lying — do NOT rest with towel under knee; TTO concomitant: different WB status and ROM per physician; flexion ≥90 deg Phase I goal; NMES with quad sets (2500 Hz, 75 bursts); no lateral patellar mobilizations; avoid dynamic knee valgus and femoral IR throughout; return to sport: >80% functional assessment prior to return-to-run program
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, brace locked PWB → WBAT, PROM flex ≥90 goal, quad sets + NMES, SLR), Phase II (2-6 wks, WBAT → FWB, progress flex full, bike wk 4, progressive strengthening), Phase III (6-10 wks, D/C brace, normalized gait, begin CKC), Phase IV (10-16 wks, advanced strengthening, jogging if criteria met), Phase V (4-6+ months, return to sport, plyometrics)
Notes: Criterion-based; TTO variant addressed (stricter WB/ROM per physician); NMES protocol parameters specified; return-to-run program included (>80% functional assessment required before initiating); no lateral patellar mobs; extensive references

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: WBAT with crutches wk 0-2 in brace locked extension; FWB by wk 2-3; D/C crutches wk 3-4
Key Restrictions: Brace locked extension x 2 wks; brace unlocked 0-90 deg wks 2-4; D/C brace wk 6; no isolated hamstrings strengthening until wk 8; no lateral patellar mobilizations; quad set progression (prone → supine → TKE); adolescent athletes may require >8 months for safe return to sport; adherence to timeline + criteria strongly recommended over timeframe only
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I Protection (0-2 wks, brace locked ext, WBAT, PROM, NMES with quad sets), Phase II (2-6 wks, brace 0-90, progressive WB/ROM, bike wk 3-4), Phase III (6-12 wks, D/C brace, progressive strengthening), Phase IV (3-6 months, advanced strengthening, begin jogging), Phase V (6-9+ months, return to sport)
Notes: .edu site; 2023 CPG format; adolescent athletes: prolonged rehab (>8 months) recommended; graft options noted: hamstring, adductor magnus, VMO, quad tendon, allograft; outcome tools: Kujala, Lysholm, IKDC, Norwich Patellar Instability Score; MPFL is primary static restraint to lateral translation during first 20-30 deg flexion; return to sport 9+ months for adolescent athletes

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dr. Theodore Ganley MD; Dr. Kathleen Maguire MD; Dr. Brendan Williams MD; Dr. J. Todd Lawrence MD PhD
WB Status: TTWB with crutches wks 1-2; WBAT wean crutches wk 2; brace locked full extension (-10 deg on brace) at all times except PROM exercises; sleep in brace locked
Key Restrictions: Brace locked extension at all times wks 1-2 (incl sleep); increase flex 10 deg/day Phase I (CPM if available: start 30 deg, increase 10 deg/daily); brace unlocked to 90 wks 2-4; D/C brace wk 6; no lateral patellar mobilizations; surgeon clearance required at 3, 6, and 9 months to progress; return to full activity at 9 months if all criteria met; earlier return to sport per surgeon based on performance
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Wks 1-2 (TTWB, brace locked, PROM/flex 10 deg/day, NMES 75 Hz, SLR in brace), Wks 2-4 (WBAT, brace 0-90, bike wk 3-4), Wks 4-8 (D/C brace wk 6, begin CKC, progressive strengthening), Wks 8-12 (advanced strengthening, begin jogging criteria-based), 3-6 months (sport-specific drills, agility), 6-9 months (full return to sport, surgeon clearance)
Notes: 2023 revised; pediatric-focused; NMES parameters: 20-50 sec OFF, 10 sec ON, 2 sec RAMP, 75 Hz, 400 µsec, 10-15 min total; home NMES 2-3x/day until no quad lag; surgeon clearance assessments at CHOP sports PT locations at 3, 6, 9 months; CPM if available; return to sport 9 months; 10 deg/day flexion increase Phase I

Source Organization: Utah Orthopedic Spine and Injury Center
WB Status: WBAT; crutches only if needed or if bilateral scope
Key Restrictions: Full ROM immediately; no brace required; full ROM allowed active flexion immediately; ice/compression/elevation initially; no formal restrictions
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (1-3 wks with stages day 1-10 and wks 2-4), Phase II Moderate Protection (5-8 wks), Phase III Controlled Activity (8-10 wks), Phase IV Return to Activity (wk 10+)
Notes: Single-surgeon protocol (Dr. Davidson); CKC exercises initiated Phase I Stage 2; pool running (forward and backward) at 8-9 wks; agility/sports-specific in Phase IV; July 2016

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): T. Ganley MD; K. Maguire MD; B. Williams MD; J.T. Lawrence MD PhD
WB Status: TTWB initially; gradually wean crutches once permitted by surgeon to WBAT
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in full ext for ambulation and sleep until surgeon permits (typically 1 wk); D/C brace when flex >=100 deg and 3 SL squats to 30 deg with good frontal plane control; respect biological healing even when pain-free early
Timeline / Phases: 5 time-based phases: Wk 0-2, Wk 2-6, Wk 6-10, Wk 10-12+, Return to Sport; gradual return to sport at 10-12 wks if criteria met
Notes: Pediatric population; 2023 revision; NMES/FES detailed parameters (75 Hz, 400 usec, 10s on/20-50s off); return to sport criteria: hop test >=90% LSI, Y-Balance >=4 cm, isokinetic >=85% LSI, IKDC >=87; ACL-RSI for psychological readiness

WB Status: WBAT with crutches; wean crutches in first several days when edema resolves, good quad activation (SLR without lag), and proper gait
Key Restrictions: No towel under knee; avoid painful activities; limit excessive walking initially; lead with non-surgical limb going up stairs, surgical limb going down
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-7 days), Phase II (day 8 - wk 2), Phase III (wks 3-4), Phase IV (wk 5+); includes return-to-run and agility/plyo programs
Notes: Criterion-based; NMES 2500 Hz 75 bursts for quad activation; bike for ROM Phase I; functional assessment >80% for return to run/sport; references included

Surgery Type: General Knee Debridement / Partial Meniscectomy / Plica Resection
Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: FWB independent of crutches when no antalgia, no increased pain/effusion, full knee ext during gait, and sufficient quad activation
Key Restrictions: Pain control and edema management priority; estim or biofeedback for quad/VMO recruitment as needed; patellar mobilization; knee flex >=90 deg Phase I
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases: Phase I (Wks 0-1), Phase II (Wks 2-6), Phase III (Return to Sport); CPG format
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; covers debridement, partial meniscectomy, and plica resection; criterion-based not time-based; outcome measures collected; references included

Surgery Type: Knee Arthroscopy (Partial Meniscectomy / Loose Body / Chondroplasty)
WB Status: Crutches as needed; D/C when swelling decreased, quad recruitment and normal gait restored
Key Restrictions: No running until Phase 3 (6+ wks); progress ROM as tolerated to restore normal motion
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks), Phase 2 (2-6 wks), Phase 3 (6+ wks)
Notes: .edu site; covers partial meniscectomy, loose body removal, and shaving chondroplasty; concise format; ROM goal 0-120 by Phase 1; plyometrics and running in Phase 3

Surgery Type: Knee Arthroscopy (Synovectomy / Chondroplasty / Loose Body / Plica)
Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: WBAT immediately post-op; no WB restrictions
Key Restrictions: No WB or ROM restrictions; full ROM allowed immediately; refer to operative note for any specific post-op recommendations; for ACL/meniscus repair use separate OSU protocols; effusion monitored throughout
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases; RTS expectation 8-16 weeks; criteria-based D/C from AD and NMES
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; 2023; covers synovectomy, chondroplasty, loose body removal, patellar impairments, knee sepsis; D/C AD criteria: full active ext, strong quad iso, 20 SLR without lag, effusion <=1+, pain-free gait; D/C NMES criteria: <20% quad deficit on HHD or isokinetic; LEFS at every visit; IKDC/KOOS/Tegner optional

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Sports Medicine Division (affiliated with Brown Alpert Medical School)
WB Status: WBAT with crutches; D/C crutches when gait normal (generally 3-5 days)
Key Restrictions: TED hose until swelling resolved; cold therapy with knee elevated and in full extension after exercise; if pain/swelling increases or ROM decreases at any time, reduce activity until resolved; patients with additional surgery progress at different rates
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (1-7 days), Phase II (~1-3 wks), Phase III (~3-6 wks), Phase IV (~6-10 wks)
Notes: ROM goal 0-90 Phase I, full ROM Phase II; ESTIM for quad activation if needed Phase I; Phase III goal: walk 2 miles at 15 min/mile pace, stair ambulation without pain/giving way; Phase IV: agility/plyometrics, running progression, transition to home/gym program; pool therapy when wound fully closed; criterion-based progression emphasized over time

Surgery Type: Partial Meniscectomy / Chondroplasty / Loose Body Removal
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopaedic Surgery, Sports Medicine and Shoulder Service; Dr. Kaar; Dr. Ali
WB Status: Crutches D/C within 1 wk when full ext, SLR without lag, and ambulates without limp
Key Restrictions: Icing 3-5x/day 30 min; no cutting/jumping/pivoting until Phase 3; swimming at 1 month; repetitions smooth and slow, NOT explosive
Timeline / Phases: 3 time-based phases: Wk 1-3, Wk 3-6, Wk 6 - 3 months; 2-3 visits/wk with 5x/wk HEP
Notes: .edu site; covers meniscectomy, chondroplasty, and loose body removal; return to sport criteria: normal strength, run without limp, full ROM, no effusion or quad atrophy; full return pending MD approval

Surgery Type: Advanced Meniscus Repair (Radial, Root, Horizontal Cleavage)
Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: Root: NWB x 4 wks, crutch D/C by 6 wks; Radial: typically NWB x 4 wks, crutch D/C by 6 wks (patient dependent); Horizontal cleavage: NWB x 2-4 wks, crutches D/C by 6 wks
Key Restrictions: Root/radial: no resisted isotonic hamstring x 8 wks; TROM immobilizer x 10-14 days (root); ROM 0-90 wks 0-2, full PROM by wk 4; symmetrical ext by wk 4 or contact surgeon; medial unloader brace x 12 months often encouraged
Timeline / Phases: Criterion-based phases (not time-based); WB/bracing varies by tear morphology; always refer to operative note
Notes: .edu site; 2023 update; CPG format; specific to radial, root, and horizontal cleavage tears (separate from standard meniscus repair); content expert and evidence-based; LEFS at each visit

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dept. of Orthopaedic Surgery, Sports Medicine and Shoulder Service; C. Sebelski PT DPT PhD OCS
WB Status: WBAT with brace locked at 0 deg
Key Restrictions: No loaded knee flex >45 deg until wk 5; no loaded flex >90 deg until wk 8; no forced hyperext if anterior horn repair; no forced flex if posterior horn repair; avoid OKC 0-30 and CKC 90-120 if PF irritation; soreness rules for progression
Timeline / Phases: 7 milestone-based phases: Wk 1-2, Wk 3-4, Wk 5-7, Wk 8-10, Wk 11-14, Wk 15-20, Wk 21+
Notes: .edu site; includes concomitant procedure modifications (ACL, MCL, PCL); NMES as needed; Alter-G treadmill; biopsychosocial factors noted; survey link for quality improvement

WB Status: PWB with crutches and brace locked x 0-2 wks; WBAT with brace locked wks 2-6 (crutches optional)
Key Restrictions: Brace locked at 0 deg for sleeping; unlocked 0-90 when NWB and awake; no squatting <90 deg x 3 months; no active hamstring curls against gravity x 3 months; no resisted lateral movement x 3 months; no stationary biking Phase I; avoid pivoting
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks), Phase II (6-12 wks), Phase III (12-16 wks), Phase IV (16+ wks)
Notes: Same surgeon as hip labral repair protocol in database; conservative approach to hamstring loading (3 months vs 8-12 wks elsewhere); light jogging at 12-16 wks if pain-free

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): J. Baumfeld MD; M. Lemos MD; L. Scola DPT OCS OMT
WB Status: WBAT with crutches and brace locked at 0 deg (Phases I-II); FWB D/C crutches and brace Phase III
Key Restrictions: PROM 0-90 deg Phases I-II; no flex beyond 90 deg until Phase III; no loaded flex >45 deg x 4 wks; no loaded flex >90 deg until 8 wks; avoid pivoting and varus/valgus stress; posterior horn repair: no HS exercises x 6 wks; complex repairs: WB restrictions extend 6-8 wks; OKC quad focus to avoid meniscus loading
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (1-4 wks), Phase II (4-6 wks), Phase III (6-12 wks), Phase IV (12-16 wks), Phase V (16+ wks)
Notes: EMS in Phase I; D/C criteria: non-antalgic gait, pain-free full ROM, no palpable edema, LE strength >=4/5, indep HEP; return to run progression: start 10 min 3x/wk, increase 1 min/session to max 30 min; speed/agility program for return to sport; swimming no frog kick; same authorship team as Lahey THA/TKA

WB Status: PWB with brace locked and crutches
Key Restrictions: Avoid active knee flexion (hamstring strain risk on posteromedial joint); no towel under knee; no pivoting on surgical side; more conservative approach for complex tears or all-inside repairs; does NOT apply to root repairs or transplants
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-3 wks), Phase II (4-5 wks), Phase III (6-8 wks), Phase IV (9-12 wks), Phase V (return to sport); includes return-to-run and agility/plyo programs
Notes: Criterion-based; NMES 2500 Hz 75 bursts; clinicians should collaborate with surgeon re: intra-op findings and repair strength; includes references

WB Status: 25% WB with crutches x 6 wks; gradual transition to FWB over 2 wks at wk 6-8
Key Restrictions: Brace 0-90 deg for ambulation and sleeping; passive flex limited to 90 deg x 6 wks; no pivoting/twisting/hopping/jumping/running until cleared; single leg exercises after 8 wks; plyometrics at 10 wks
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks), Phase 2 (2-6 wks), Phase 3 (6-12 wks), Phase 4 (12+ wks)
Notes: .edu site; root repair-specific (more conservative than standard meniscus repair); Russian stim for quad recruitment; aquatic therapy with MD clearance; slide board after 10 wks

WB Status: WBAT with hinged knee brace locked in extension Phase I; FWB with brace locked in extension
Key Restrictions: Hinged brace locked in extension at all times (ambulating, sleeping, standing) Phase I; no active knee extension Phase I; no passive flexion >60 deg Phase I — do not push motion; no flexion >90 deg Phase II; no active knee extension until wk 6; flexion limit progression: 60 deg (wk 0-2), 90 deg (wk 2-6); tissue quality and repair strength affect progression — check with surgeon
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-14 days, brace locked extension, WBAT, PROM ≤60 deg, heel slides, quad sets, glute sets), Phase II (2-6 wks, progress flexion to 90 deg, AAROM, stationary bike wk 4, begin active extension wk 6), Phase III (6-10 wks, full ROM goal, progressive strengthening), Phase IV (10-16 wks, advanced strengthening, jogging if criteria met), Phase V (16+ wks, return to sport)
Notes: Criterion-based; covers both patellar AND quad tendon repair in same protocol; brace locked extension at all times Phase I is the critical restriction; no active extension Phase I; flexion progression explicit: ≤60 (Phase I) → ≤90 (Phase II) → full (Phase III); heel props and prone hangs for extension from day 1

WB Status: WBAT in hinged brace locked in extension; D/C brace at 6-8 wks
Key Restrictions: Brace locked extension x 6-8 wks; no active knee extension x 6 wks; PROM flex guided by op note; quad tendon rupture: usually age >40 (88%); patellar tendon rupture: usually age <40 (avg 28); graft tissue may be added for desired length; in rare cases end-to-end repair performed
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks, brace locked ext, PROM per surgeon, quad sets, SLR, hip strengthening), Phase II (6-12 wks, D/C brace, progressive ROM/strengthening, CKC emphasis), Phase III (12+ wks, advanced strengthening, sport-specific, return to function)
Notes: Covers both quad and patellar tendon repair + partial tears; age demographics noted (quad >40, patellar <40); conservative management for small partial tears; graft augmentation and end-to-end variants noted; concise patient-friendly format

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry PT DPT LAT CSCS; UW Health Sports Medicine
WB Status: WBAT in brace locked in extension; FWB x 6 wks in brace
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension for ambulation x 6 wks; brace locked in extension for sleeping x 6 wks; no active knee extension x 6 wks; PROM flex: ≤45 deg wks 0-2, ≤60 deg wks 2-4, ≤90 deg wks 4-6; no OKC knee extension until wk 12; no deep squats or lunges until wk 16; avoid patellar compression exercises early
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, brace locked ext, WBAT, PROM ≤45 deg), Phase II (2-6 wks, progress flex 60 → 90 deg, multi-angle quad isometrics, stationary bike wk 4-6), Phase III (6-12 wks, D/C brace, full ROM, CKC strengthening, no OKC ext until wk 12), Phase IV (12-16+ wks, progressive strengthening, jogging at wk 16, sport-specific)
Notes: UW Health / developed with Marc Sherry PT DPT; flexion progression: 45 → 60 → 90 deg over 6 wks (most conservative early flexion of all protocols); no OKC knee extension until wk 12; illustrated with exercise photos

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Sports Medicine Division (affiliated with Brown Alpert Medical School)
WB Status: WBAT with crutches and brace locked in extension; D/C crutches when gait normal
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension for ambulation and sleep; no active knee extension Phase I; PROM flex: ≤60 deg Phase I (0-2 wks); no lifting with operative LE; TED hose until swelling resolved
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, brace locked ext, WBAT, PROM ≤60 deg, quad sets with ESTIM, SLR x 4, heel slides), Phase II (~2-6 wks, progress flex to 90 deg, AAROM/AROM, stationary bike), Phase III (~6-10 wks, progressive strengthening, proprioception), Phase IV (~10-16 wks, agility/plyometrics, running progression, return to sport)
Notes: Brown University affiliated; TED hose for DVT prevention; ESTIM for quad activation if difficulty initiating contraction; similar structure to their knee arthroscopy protocol

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: FWB with brace locked in extension x 6 wks; typically WB with TROM open x 1-2 wks prior to discharge from brace
Key Restrictions: No flexion >90 deg x 6 wks; no forced flexion x 8-10 wks; no loaded CKC quad strengthening x 6 wks; OKC progression: multi-angle isometrics at wk 4, LAQ at wk 6 (add weight as able), knee extension machine at wk 8 (partial → full); refer to operative note for ROM guidelines; long-term quad strength deficits are common — can exhaust insurance-approved PT visits
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I Protection (0-2 wks, brace locked ext, PROM ≤60 deg, quad sets with NMES), Phase II (2-6 wks, progress flexion to 90 deg, multi-angle isometrics wk 4, LAQ wk 6, stationary bike wk 4), Phase III (6-12 wks, D/C brace, full ROM, knee ext machine wk 8, progressive strengthening), Phase IV (12+ wks, power/endurance, return to function/sport)
Notes: .edu site; CPG format; April 2020; covers both quad AND patellar tendon repair; risk factors noted: long-term quad deficits, insurance visit exhaustion; OKC progression timeline explicit (isometrics wk 4 → LAQ wk 6 → machine wk 8); outcome tools: LEFS, Lysholm; Cho-Pat strap/patellar taping for PF symptoms; criterion-based

WB Status: Per surgeon's instructions; commonly WBAT
Key Restrictions: Avoid deep squats, pivoting, impact activity; limit ambulation to 700 steps/day initially; no pillow/towel roll under knee; avoid twisting/pivoting; limit prolonged sitting/standing
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-7 days), Phase II (1-4 wks), Phase III (4-8 wks), Phase IV (8-12 wks), Phase V (3-6 months)
Notes: ROM goals: >70 deg flex Phase I, >90 Phase II, >110 Phase III, >120 Phase IV; NMES parameters specified; criterion-based with strength LSI targets (>=80%); includes references

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): R. Ghazinouri PT; Dept. of Rehabilitation Services
WB Status: WBAT with AD (unless otherwise indicated by surgeon)
Key Restrictions: No exercises with weights/resistance initially; avoid torque/twisting forces across knee especially when WB; nothing behind operative knee; towel roll at ankle for extension
Timeline / Phases: 3+ phases: Phase I (Day 0-3), Phase II (Day 3 - Wk 6, with Wk 1-4 and Wk 4-6 sub-phases), Phase III (6-12 wks)
Notes: ROM goals: -10 to 80 deg Phase I, >=0-110 Phase II; NMES for quad if poor contraction; CPM not standard but may be indicated; wean AD by end of 2nd week; detailed pain management section; multimodal analgesia

WB Status: WBAT with device unless otherwise noted by surgical team
Key Restrictions: Avoid torque/twisting forces through knee especially when WB; no exercises with weights/resistance initially; nothing behind operative knee; towel roll at ankle for extension; towel roll at trochanter for neutral alignment
Timeline / Phases: 3+ phases: Phase I (POD 0-3), Phase II (Day 3 - Wk 6, with early Wk 0-3 and late Wk 3-6 sub-phases), additional phases
Notes: ROM goals: >=80 flex and <=-10 ext Phase I, >=0-110 Phase II; revision TKA progress Phases I-II cautiously; updated 2022 version of BWH protocol; detailed perioperative pain management (spinal, general, adductor canal block)

WB Status: WBAT unless modified WB indicated by physician
Key Restrictions: Avoid torquing/twisting motions of knee; no pillow under knee; revision TKA progress with more caution; communicate with MD regarding kneeling, running, high-impact restrictions
Timeline / Phases: 6 phases: Phase I (pre-op), Phase II (inpatient), Phase III (0-3 wks, 4-6 visits), Phase IV (3-6 wks, 3-6 visits), Phase V (6-12 wks), Phase VI (12+ wks); 7-12 total visits expected
Notes: System-level guideline; criterion-based; ROM goals: 5-90 deg Phase II, 5-100 Phase III, 0-110 Phase IV; NMES/biofeedback recommended; manipulation flagged if ROM not progressing by wk 4-5; includes outcome measures (WOMAC, KOOS JR)

WB Status: WBAT
Key Restrictions: No pillow under knee; monitor wound healing and DVT; soreness rule: <2 hrs post-exercise; <2 cm swelling increase with exercise
Timeline / Phases: 4 rehab phases + preop visit; Phase I (immediate post-op), Phase II (motion/mobility), Phase III, Phase IV; most patients completing Phase IV by week 12
Notes: ROM milestones: 0-90 by hospital D/C, manipulation considered if not past 90 by wk 6; NMES emphasized for quad activation; detailed progression criteria (2x8 reps without pain increase); pre-op visit includes NMES education

Source Organization: South Shore Orthopedics / South Shore Health
WB Status: WBAT; AD required minimum 6 wks (walker to crutches to cane)
Key Restrictions: No specific ROM restrictions listed; avoid high-impact activities and repeated pivoting/twisting after 6 wks
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase 1 (Day 0 - D/C), Phase 2 (D/C - 6 wks), Phase 3 (6-12 wks), Phase 4 (12+ wks); 4-6 month full recovery typical, up to 9-12 months
Notes: Comprehensive patient education document; driving guidelines (6 wks R knee, 4 wks L knee); D/C at 70-80% functional level; outpatient PT typically starts ~3 wks post-op

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): L. Specht MD; J. Agrillo PT; S. Barrera OT; M. Dynan PT
WB Status: Generally WBAT with AD for primary TKA, unless otherwise indicated by MD
Key Restrictions: Towel roll at ankle to promote knee ext and prevent heel ulcers; nothing under knee; monitor DVT (swelling, erythema, calf pain); monitor wound for infection
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-1 wk, hospital, PT 2x/day), Phase II (1-3 wks, transitional), Phase III (3-6 wks, OP early), Phase IV (6-12 wks, OP intermediate), Phase V (3+ months)
Notes: ROM goals: full ext at 0 deg and flex to 90 Phase I, 90-120 Phase II, progressing toward 0-120 Phase III; NMES for quad re-education Phase III; D/C criteria: no pain with ADLs, LE strength >=4/5, indep reciprocal stairs; OT consultation; same authorship as Lahey THA protocol

WB Status: WBAT
Key Restrictions: No pillow under knee at any point; no sleeping in recliner with knees bent; towel/pillow behind calf for extension; emphasize terminal knee ext stretching at 3 wks if not straight
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-3 days), Phase II (3 days - 4 wks), Phase III (4-8 wks), Phase IV (8-12 wks); full functional return ~12 wks
Notes: ROM goals: 0-90 PROM Phase I, 0-120 AROM Phase II, symmetrical Phase III; manipulation if not 5-90 by wk 4 or 0-90 by wk 6; Quad Index targets: 85% Phase III, 90% Phase IV; criterion-based

WB Status: WBAT with walker progressing to WBQC then straight cane
Key Restrictions: CPM 0-100 deg started in recovery room min 4 hrs; increase ~10 deg/day; towel roll under ankle when CPM not in use; do not allow knees forward of toes in wall slides; limit sitting to 45 min single session
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase I (Wk 1, day-by-day POD 1-5), Phase II (Wks 2-5), Phase III (Wks 6-8)
Notes: ROM goals: 0-70 POD1, 0-80 POD2, 0-90 POD3, 0-95 POD4, 0-100 POD5, 0-115 Wk 2-3, 0-120 Wk 3-4, 0-125 Wk 4-5; CPM use is standard; includes pre-op PT session; very detailed day-by-day inpatient progression

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center

Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-3 wks, PWB, circumduction, bike no resistance), Phase II (4-7 wks, progressive WB to FWB, aquatic, hip strengthening), Phase III (8-11 wks, advanced strengthening, sport-specific), Phase IV (12+ wks, return to sport); labral repair: Phase II starts wk 4; osteoplasty only: Phase II starts wk 2
Notes: .edu site; CPG format 2019; covers osteoplasty, labral repair, labral augmentation, labral reconstruction, capsular repair, capsular plication; outcome tools: mHHS, iHOT-33, LEFS, FAAM; return to sport criteria: LSI ≥90%, Vail Sport Test, physician clearance; references 23 citations

Surgery Type: Hip Arthroscopy for FAI with Labral Repair
WB Status: PWB 20 lbs foot-flat step-to with crutches wks 0-2; wean crutches wk 3+
Key Restrictions: No active SLR; no active hip flexion x 21 days (self-assisted only); hip flexion 0-90 deg, ext 0 deg (no beyond neutral), ABD 0-30, ER 0-30, IR 0-30 Phase I; no grade III-IV hip mobs; no long axis hip distraction (labral repair or reconstruction); avoid sitting >30 min x 2 wks; sit with hip angle <90 deg; prone lying 15 min 2-3x/day to avoid hip flexor contracture; capsular closure: protect ER and extension; labral reconstruction: stricter timeline than repair; avoid hip flexor tendinitis, synovitis, trochanteric bursitis, LBP/SI pain throughout
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, PWB 20 lbs, ROM within limits, circumduction for adhesion prevention), Phase II (2-5 wks, progressive WB, stationary bike, aquatic therapy wk 4+), Phase III (6-8 wks, full WB, progressive strengthening, sport-specific initiation), Phase IV (9-12 wks, return to sport); labral reconstruction: add 2-4 weeks to each phase
Notes: Criterion-based; capsular closure and capsular plication variants addressed; labral repair vs reconstruction differentiated (reconstruction = longer timeline); circumduction critical for adhesion prevention (risk factors: age <30, mHHS <50, no microfracture, no circumduction); outcome tools: mHHS, iHOT-33, VAS; return to sport: HAGOS ≥90%, LSI ≥90% strength, physician clearance

Notes: 2020; extensive evidence-based rationale provided for each protocol decision; gluteal isometrics decrease iliopsoas spasm (cited evidence); adhesion risk factors explicitly listed; capsular integrity correlated to improved outcomes (cited); labral repair vs reconstruction timeline differentiated; illustrated exercise photos included

Notes: US Ski Team physician; PT starts wk 1 (earliest of all protocols here); X-ray progression criterion throughout; pool at wk 3; pec minor stretching Phase III for scapular protraction; posterior scapular stretching wk 8 if needed; return to work/sport: bilateral AROM + 4+/5 strength

Source Organization: Dr. Jonathan Watson MD (Silver Cross Hospital / Advocate Christ Medical Center)
WB Status: Sling immobilization x 6 wks at all times except bathing and ROM exercises; ROM precautions: FF 130, ER 30 Phase I (no active ROM); no restrictions Phase II
Key Restrictions: No AROM Phase I; ROM limits: FF 130 + ER 30 Phase I; no lifting/carrying Phase I; no ROM restrictions Phase II; no lifting >2 lbs Phase II; no overhead lifting Phase III; no power clean/dead lift/back squats Phase III; fracture healing confirmed before Phase IV; post-activity soreness resolves within 24 hrs Phase IV
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-6 wks, PROM + AAR at wk 3; biceps/triceps isotonics + deltoid scapular plane wk 3; ER/IR isometrics + table slides + serratus punch wk 4; stationary bike in sling at wk 3), Phase II (7-12 wks, no ROM restrictions; active ROM; GH/scapular mobs; Theraband ER/IR/flex/ABD wk 8; biceps/triceps wk 8), Phase III (13-18 wks, scapular + cuff strength; walk/jog/run progression wk 12; no overhead lifting), Phase IV (19+ wks, return to sport; post-activity soreness ≤24 hrs)
Notes: Criterion-based; deltoid isotonics in scapular plane starts wk 3 (earlier than most); serratus punch at wk 4; higher level athletes: single LE balance with head movements, functional squats, step-ups during Phase II; kinetic chain integration (LE and core) emphasized throughout; walk/jog/run starts wk 12

Source Organization: Dr. Katherine J. Coyner MD (UConn Musculoskeletal Institute)
WB Status: Sling at all times x 2 wks including nighttime; may D/C at wk 4 if comfortable; inpatient 0-4 days then outpatient

Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Inpatient (0-4 days, sling; pendulums; PROM FF 90 + ER 30; home program), Phase 1 OP (hospital D/C-wk 4, sling at all times x 2 wks; PROM FF 90 + ER 30; scapular retraction; postural correction), Phase 2 (wks 4-8, sling D/C if comfortable; self-assisted FF 90 + 20 deg increments/wk; ER 40; grade I-II mobs; no cuff strengthening; hydrotherapy wk 3+ within ROM limits), Phase 3 (wks 8-12, progressive full FF and ER; IR behind back begins; grade III-IV mobs; posterior capsule + pec minor stretching; submaximal isometric RTC; manual resistance scapular), Phase 4 (wks 12+, progressive isotonic RTC; functional activities)
Notes: UConn academic medical center protocol; notable precaution: no NSAIDs x 6 wks (fracture healing concern); inpatient phase explicitly described (0-4 days); no active IR x 6 wks; hydrotherapy permitted at wk 3; IR behind back explicitly starts Phase 3 (wk 8); FF progresses 20 deg/wk from 90 deg starting wk 4; pec minor and posterior capsule stretching Phase 3

Source Organization: Dr. Grant S. Garrigues MD (Rush University Medical Center)
WB Status: Sling with abduction pillow at all times except exercises (3-5x/day), showering, and dressing; D/C at wk 6
Key Restrictions: Intraoperative safe zones established by surgeon (two programs): 140/40 Program (FF 140 + ER 40) or 130/30 Program (FF 130 + ER 30); stay within surgeon-defined safe zone Phase I; no ABD; no aggressive or forced PROM until radiographic union; ROM before strengthening emphasized; biceps tenodesis co-procedure: no resisted elbow flexion x 6 wks, support well arm for elbow flexion/extension wks 1-2; no sudden forceful resisted IR (golf, swimming) until >3 months
Timeline / Phases: 2 phases + return to activity: Phase I Passive Motion (0-6 wks, PROM within safe zone per surgeon: FF 140/130 + ER 40/30; pendulums; scapular retraction; active elbow/wrist/hand AROM; ice 20-30 min as much as possible first 8-10 days), Phase II Active ROM (6-10 wks, full ROM by wk 10 if radiographic healing; begin AROM/AAROM; side-lying ER; Theraband 2 lbs after passive motion restored; scapulothoracic strengthening; aquatic therapy earliest 1 month if wound healed), Return to Activity Phase (wks 10+, sports/occupation specific)
Notes: Single-surgeon protocol; two distinct intraoperative safe zone programs (140/40 vs 130/30); radiographic healing required before advancing Phase II; full ROM goal by wk 10; sleeper stretch and hand slide IR at wk 6; prone T scapular strengthening; aquatic PT at 1 month minimum; October 2018

Source Organization: University of Colorado / Denver Shoulder Surgeon
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dr. Adam Seidl MD; Dept. of Orthopedics, Anschutz Medical Campus

Notes: .edu affiliated (U of Colorado Anschutz); PT starts wk 2; sling includes at night; ER progresses 20 deg/wk from wk 5; anterior deltoid and scapular stabilization emphasized Phase 3; upper trapezius/serratus anterior force couple emphasized; UBE low resistance Phase 3; aerobic conditioning throughout; modalities for pain/inflammation Phase 2

WB Status: Sling at all times except exercises Phase I; wean per tolerance

WB Status: Sling in neutral rotation with abduction pillow (30-45 deg) wks 0-3; wean sling Phase II (4-6 wks); discontinue sling Phase III (7-8 wks)

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Reg B. Wilcox III PT; Dept. of Rehabilitation Services
WB Status: Sling/immobilizer 4-6 wks (4 wks isolated rTSA; 6 wks with tendon transfer or subscapularis repair); no shoulder AROM Phase I
Key Restrictions: Dislocation precautions x 10-12 wks: avoid shoulder extension beyond neutral AND combination of adduction + extension + IR; no lifting with operative UE; no supporting body weight with operative arm; keep elbow supported in supine ('always visualize elbow while on back'); if tendon transfer: PROM/AAROM start closer to wk 6, not earlier; subscapularis repair: delay accordingly; no soaking incision 2 wks; no swimming/jacuzzi/wading

Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: Sling 4-6 wks per surgeon; no driving x 6 wks; jogging may begin at 12 wks
Key Restrictions: No IR, cross-body adduction, or extension x 12 wks (Hand Behind Back position); elevation in scaption only 4-6 wks (rTSA most stable at 30 deg ER); no forced shoulder flexion; no stretching into pain; no pushing to end ranges; no supporting body weight x 12 wks; long-term: no push-ups or bench press; 15 lb limit below shoulder height; 10 lb limit above shoulder height; subscapularis precautions: no ER PROM past neutral x 4 wks, no active IR x 8 wks (confirm with surgeon)
Timeline / Phases: 4+ phases: Phase I (0-2 wks), Phase II (2-4 wks), Phase III (4-6 wks), Phase IV (6-12 wks), strengthening/plyometric phases (12-16 wks+); plyometrics no earlier than 16 wks; return to sport at 6 months with physician clearance
Notes: .edu site; 2023 CPG format; outcome tools: QuickDASH, Simple Shoulder Test, ASES Short Form; discharge criteria: 110-150 deg elevation, 30-50 deg functional ER; return to sport criteria explicit; most common sports: swimming, fitness, golf, tennis, pickleball; posterior instability precautions check with surgeon

WB Status: Sling day and night x 4 wks; discontinue sling Phase II (wk 4-8)
Key Restrictions: ER limited to 30 deg (no forceful motion) Phase I; IR limited to belt line Phase I; ABD limited to 90 deg Phase I; no active ROM of involved arm Phase I; no lifting/WB Phase I; no shoulder ER with arm at 90 deg ABD Phase II; avoid forceful ER stretching; no lifting/resisted exercises Phase II; no lifting >5 lbs Phase III; key note: subscapularis repair comes under tension during ER and forward elevation past 90 deg — these motions restricted more than for superior RC tear
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (0-4 wks, PROM/protection), Phase II (4-8 wks, AAROM/AROM, D/C sling), Phase III (8-12 wks, initial strengthening, no lifting >5 lbs), Phase IV (12-16 wks, progressive strengthening), Phase V (16+ wks, return to function/sport)
Notes: Standalone subscapularis protocol (distinct from supraspinatus repair); criterion-based; scapular clock in sidelying starts at wk 2; ER with arm at 45 deg ABD introduced Phase II; no ER at 90 deg ABD until Phase III+; IR strengthening introduced cautiously late; tissue quality, repair size, secondary procedures, pre-op ROM all affect progression; references Altintas et al 2019 IJSPT as primary rehab reference

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair — Large to Massive Tears (>3 cm)
WB Status: Sling with abduction pillow (30-45 deg) day and night x 6 wks; PROM begins at wk 6 (not earlier); no active shoulder ROM until wks 10-14
Key Restrictions: 6-week period of full immobilization — NO PROM of shoulder Phase I (wks 1-6); only hand/wrist/elbow AROM (no active elbow x 4 wks if biceps tenodesis); no pushing/pulling; no overhead or behind back; no weight bearing through arm; subscapularis concomitant repair: no ER past 0 deg, no ABD past 90 deg Phase I; no active ROM despite minimal pain (Phase II); no IR behind back Phase II; no aggressive or painful PROM; no sudden/jerking motions Phase III; no loading healing tendon
Timeline / Phases: 5 phases: Phase I (wks 1-6, full immobilization — NO ROM), Phase II (wks 6-10, PROM begins with PT: FF ≤100 deg, ER ≤30 deg, ABD ≤90 deg), Phase III AAROM (wks 10-14), Phase III AROM (wks 14-18), Phase IV strengthening (wks 18+), Phase V return to function
Notes: Key differentiator from small-medium: full immobilization wks 1-6 with zero shoulder ROM; PROM starts wk 6 not wk 0-3; AAROM not until wk 10; isometric strengthening not until wks 14-18 (submaximal only — maximal effort can overload repair); PROM criteria to advance: ≥100-120 deg FF, ≥25-45 deg ER, ≥90 deg ABD; pendulums DO NOT actively use shoulder muscles; scapular exercises begin wk 6 without sling

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair — Large to Massive Tears (>3 cm)
Source Organization: Ohio State University Wexner Medical Center
WB Status: Sling 6 wks (vs 4-6 wks for small-medium); PT starts at wks 4-6
Key Restrictions: Large 3-5 cm; massive >5 cm; subscapularis repair (12 wks): no ER >30 deg, no cross-body ADD, no active IR or IR behind back, no supporting body weight; superior capsule reconstruction: follow protocol timeframes + lifting limit 5 lbs x 6 months; no PROM into pain; risk factors for poor outcomes: low preoperative function, poor preoperative active ER, younger age, lower education, workers comp claims
Timeline / Phases: 3 phases: Phase 1 Protection (wks 4-6, PROM flexion + ER, scapular isotonic), Phase 2 (wks 6-12, AAROM wks 6-8, AROM wks 8-12, sub-max isometrics, UBE, closed chain), Phase 3 (wks 12-16+, T-band, prone strengthening, rhythmic stabilization, return to sport/work)
Notes: .edu site; large 3-5 cm, massive >5 cm (explicit definitions); covers superior capsule reconstruction variant (5 lb limit x 6 months); subscapularis precautions same as small-medium protocol; manual therapy: posterior/caudal GH mobs wks 4-6; outcome tools: DASH, QuickDASH; discharge criteria: full AROM no scapular substitution, 5/5 MMT RTC, 65-70% IR/ER isokinetic; risk factors table included; seated press-ups wks 10-12; quadruped weight shifts

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair — Massive Tears (>5 cm) / Type III Protocol
WB Status: Shoulder immobilizer x 8 wks (worn at all times except ROM exercises; D/C completely at wk 8; worn at night wks 0-6); no active motion x 8 wks; no active ABD x 10 wks
Key Restrictions: Massive defined as >5 cm; immobilizer 8 full weeks; no PROM until wk 2-6 phase; no active motion x 8 wks; no active ABD x 10 wks; no ER resistance in ABD/scaption x 12 wks; subscapularis repair: ER limited to 20 deg (vs 30 deg standard) wks 3-4, no IR isometrics; no swimming, running, or jumping through Phase III (12 wks); avoid impact aerobic conditioning throughout
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-2 wks, immobilizer, pendulums + distal AROM only), Phase II (2-6 wks, PROM: FF ≤110 wk 3-4 → 130 wk 5-6; ER ≤30 deg at side; subscapularis: ER ≤20 deg; sub-max isometrics), Phase III (6-12 wks, AROM wk 8, active ABD wk 10, isotonic IR/ER, scapular/core), Phase IV (12 wks-6 months, progressive strengthening, sport-specific); PT starts 3-5 days post-op 1-2x/wk
Notes: Single-surgeon protocol (Dr. Bott); 2023; also indicated for exceedingly poor tissue quality regardless of tear size; explicit PROM ROM milestones per week (FF 110 wk 3-4, 130 wk 5-6); progression criteria: FF 130 deg + ER 45 deg at side by wk 6; Phase IV initiation of supraspinatus/infraspinatus strengthening at months 3-4; subscapularis variant explicitly noted in Phase II with reduced ER limit

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair — Small to Medium Tears (<1–4 cm)
WB Status: Sling 24 hrs/day x 4-6 wks; remove for bathing/dressing/exercises; keep arm relaxed in sling
Key Restrictions: No AROM or resisted ROM Phase I (wks 0-4); no IR or extension initially — all PROM starts in scapular plane; no behind back movements (avoid combined ext/add/IR); no lifting/carrying; no pushing/pulling; subscapularis repair: limit extension to neutral x 6 wks, ER to neutral x 4 wks then >neutral to tolerance until wk 6, no isolated IR resistance x 12 wks; open repair: limit extension/ER to neutral x 6 wks, no active flexion x 6-8 wks, no IR resistance x 6-8 wks (deltoid detachment); Regeneten augmentation: sling 4 wks, AROM at wk 4, strengthening at wk 8; no aggressive strengthening x 12 wks; strength goal 85-90% by 5-6 months
Timeline / Phases: 4 phases: Phase I (0-4 wks, max protected PROM/safe AAROM), Phase II (4-8 wks, AROM begins wk 6, sub-max isometrics), Phase III (8-12 wks, progressive strengthening), Phase IV (12+ wks, advanced/return to sport/work); strength training should continue ≥1 year post-op
Notes: Evidence-based; outlined for double-row suture bridge (trans-osseous) supraspinatus repair; covers arthroscopic and mini-open; includes separate sub-protocols for: subscapularis repair, open repair, and Regeneten augmentation; pre-op stiffness correlated to post-op stiffness (best predictor: pre-op IR behind back vertebral level); blood flow restriction (BFR) exercises noted as optional; create posterior-dominant shoulder (emphasize ER and scapular retractors); no pulleys wks 0-4; small <1 cm, medium 2-4 cm (their definitions); large/massive 5+ cm

Notes: Surgeon protocol; patellar glides 4x/day wks 1-6; return to sport: painless ROM = contralateral, no effusion, quad control, excellent agility/proprioception; Get Set Train Smarter app recommended; functional brace evidence-based for contact football/soccer/rugby

WB Status: WBAT with crutches wk 1 (balance only); encourage FWB ASAP; advance per tolerance
Key Restrictions: Knee immobilizer 48 hrs only (exception: concomitant MCL tear); avoid all open chain x 12 wks; open chain max 25 lbs wks 17-24; hamstring bridge program begins wk 16; functional brace for contact sports only
Timeline / Phases: Week 1 (limited activity, WBAT, SLR 50 2x/day); Wks 2-8 (ROM: 0-120 wk 4, 0-130+ wk 6; CKC when no limp); Wks 9-12 (aggressive ROM, aerobic); Wks 13-16 (jogging/swimming/golf with MD OK at wk 12, plyometrics); Wks 17-24 (OC up to 25 lbs, retro training); Wk 25+ (return to sport)
Notes: Hamstring graft variant: jogging begins wk 12 (vs wk 8 BTB), bridge program delayed to wk 16, OC begins wk 17; return to sport: painless ROM = contralateral, no effusion, quad control, excellent agility/proprioception; Get Set Train Smarter app recommended

WB Status: PWB 40-60 lbs with crutches wks 0-2; PWB 80-100 lbs wks 3-4; D/C crutches after wk 4
Key Restrictions: Knee immobilizer full time wks 0-2; NO SLR wks 0-2; CKC before OC quad strengthening; McConnell taping if patellofemoral pain present wks 9-12; return to sport requires MD clearance
Timeline / Phases: Wks 0-2 (PWB 40-60 lbs, immobilizer, quad sets/ankle pumps, well leg bike/UE ergometer); Wks 3-4 (D/C immobilizer, AROM, crutches PWB 80-100 lbs, foot/ankle mobility); Wks 5-8 (formal PT, CKC quads, SLR, patellar mobs, OC hams, D/C crutches wk 4, hip mobility/LE control); Wks 9-12 (OC quads/hams, McConnell taping if needed); Wks 13-24 (HEP 3x/wk, return to sport with MD ok)
Notes: Fulkerson/Elmslie-Trillat osteotomy for patellar maltracking; hip mobility and LE control emphasized mid-phase; patellar mobilization central post-op; no specific RTS functional criteria listed - MD clearance required

WB Status: PWB 40-60 lbs with crutches wks 0-4; WBAT unprotected when no limp and good quad control (wks 4-6+)
Key Restrictions: Knee immobilizer full time wks 0-2; no resistance exercises wks 4-6; no kneeling or squatting until after wk 8; does NOT apply to ACL + meniscal repair combined (follow ACL protocol); return to sport with MD ok after minimum 4 months
Timeline / Phases: Wks 0-4 (PWB 40-60 lbs, immobilizer wks 0-2 then D/C, AROM as tolerated, quad sets/SLR/ankle pumps, well leg bike); Wks 4-6 (WBAT when no limp/quad control, AROM stretches, goal full ROM by wk 6, stationary bike no resistance); Wks 6-8 (initiate CKC strengthening, no kneeling/squatting); Wks 8-12 (initiate open chain strengthening); Wks 12-16 (protected agility and sports-specific); return to sport after 4 months with MD ok
Notes: Explicitly for isolated meniscal repair only; ACL + meniscal repair patients follow ACL protocol; RTS minimum 4 months with MD clearance; no specific functional RTS criteria listed

WB Status: WBAT with crutches wk 1 (balance only); encourage FWB ASAP
Key Restrictions: Knee brace locked in full extension x 2 wks; transition out of brace wks 3-4; D/C brace by wk 4; avoid all open chain x 12 wks; open chain limited to 5 lbs initially at wks 13-16
Timeline / Phases: Wk 1 (WBAT, brace locked extension, SLR 50 2x/day, ankle pumps, ted hose); Wks 2-6 (ROM: full extension by POD 7, 0-130 by wk 4; CKC when no limp, no OC x 12 wks, well leg/UE conditioning); Wks 7-8 (aggressive ROM, aerobic); Wks 9-12 (jogging/golf/swimming with MD OK at wk 8); Wks 13-16 (OC 5 lbs max if full painless ROM); return to sport with MD clearance
Notes: Similar framework to ACL protocol; brace locked 0 deg x 2 wks then weaned; open chain begins wk 13 at 5 lb max; return to sport: full painless ROM, no effusion, quad control, excellent agility/proprioception

WB Status: PWB with crutches x 6 wks; progressive WB wks 6-12; FWB Phase III+
Key Restrictions: Brace locked 0/0 deg x 6-8 wks; ROM limited 0-90 deg passive prone flexion or seated (anterior tibial translation technique) x 6 wks; no resisted hamstrings x 12 wks; minimize posterior tibial translation throughout; quad sets with towel behind tibia
Timeline / Phases: Phase I Max Protection (wks 0-6): PWB, brace locked, patellar mobs, ROM 0-90, quad sets/SLR; Phase II Progressive (wks 6-12): brace open 0-90, progress ROM, gym strengthening (leg press/calf/abd-add - NO hamstrings), bilateral CKC squats, stationary bike, pool; Phase III Advanced (wks 12-20): full ROM, advance cardio, begin resisted hamstrings; Phase IV Return to Sport (wks 20 to 9-12 mo): pool running wk 20, dry land running wk 24, agility drills 7-8 mo, return to sport 9-12 mo
Notes: Quadriceps-dominant protocol; posterior tibial shear avoided throughout; resisted hamstrings held until wk 12; pool running precedes dry land; return to sport 9-12 months; anterior tibial translation technique during early ROM protects graft

WB Status: NWB with crutches x 6 wks; progressive WB wks 6-12; FWB Phase III+
Key Restrictions: Brace locked 0 deg x 6 wks; ROM 30-90 deg WITH VALGUS STRESS APPLIED wks 0-4; NO hyperextension at any phase; limit flexion to 90 deg until wk 4; no varus stress; functional brace fitting at wk 20+; sports test required for return to competition
