Key Restrictions: Sling 6 weeks; PROM only weeks 1-6; no active UE PREs initially; no throwing/overhead activities until 4+ months (16+ weeks)
Timeline / Phases: Wks 1-3: sling 6 wks, passive supine elevation with opposite hand, passive ER to neutral, hand/wrist/elbow PREs, scapular strengthening, deltoid isometrics; Wks 3-6: advance PROM (elevation to tolerance, ER to 30°), sub-maximal IR/ER isometrics at wk 5, scapular PREs; Wks 6-9: Theraband IR/ER, deltoid isotonics, biceps PREs, RC isotonics, large muscle group PREs (pecs/lats), isokinetics IR/ER with eccentric emphasis; Wks 9-16: aggressive UE PREs, IR/ER velocity spectrum isokinetics, plyometrics for overhead athletes, throwing/racquet program; 16+ wks: functional exercises, isokinetics, plyometrics, sport-specific rehab
Notes: Sling for 6 weeks; passive elevation only first 3 weeks (opposite hand assist); IR/ER isometrics begin week 5; deltoid isotonics and Theraband at week 6; isokinetics with eccentric emphasis from week 6; plyometrics for overhead athletes from week 9; sport-specific activities from week 16; no throwing until 4+ months

Surgery Type: Latarjet Procedure (Coracoid Transfer for Anterior Shoulder Instability)
WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling at all times (remove only for shower with arm at side); sleep in sling with towel under elbow; no AROM of operative shoulder initially; no excessive ER ROM — respect anterior capsule; no lifting; no throwing/overhead until 4 months; avoid triceps dips, wide-grip bench press, military press, lat pulls behind head permanently after Latarjet
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (wks 1-3): sling always, PROM (FF/abd to tolerance, IR 45° at 30° abd, ER 0-25° in scapular plane), scapular clock/isometrics, ball squeezes, cryotherapy; Phase II (wks 4-9): advance PROM, wean sling wks 6-7, AAROM/AROM to 90-110° with good mechanics, rhythmic stabilization, scapular strengthening, IR/ER tubing; Phase III (wks 10-15): normalize strength/NM control, chest-level activities, biceps/subscapularis strengthening, gradual pec progression (avoid anterior capsule stress); Phase IV (wks 16-20): full ROM/strength, overhead activities if cleared, plyometrics/interval sports with PT and MD clearance
Notes: Phase II criteria to advance: 100° passive FF, 30° passive ER; Phase III criteria to advance: PROM FF ≥155°, AROM ≥145°, ER within 8-10° of contralateral at 20° abd; avoid "empty can"/IR in scaption due to impingement risk; avoid triceps dips, wide-grip bench, military press, lat pulls behind head; no throwing or overhead athletic moves until 4 months; 4 phases

WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling at all times (except HEP and PT) for 6 weeks; sleep in sling; no lifting with operative shoulder; passive FF limited to 130° weeks 0-4; passive ER limited to 25° weeks 4-6; avoid active IR and horizontal adduction through week 12; avoid anterior capsule stretching throughout; no overhead work
Timeline / Phases: 0-4 wks: sling always, pendulums, passive supine FF to 130°, hand/wrist/elbow ROM, peri-scapular strengthening; 4-6 wks: sling continues, PROM (FF full, ER 25°), deltoid/RC isometrics, avoid anterior capsule stretch; 6-12 wks: discontinue sling wk 6, PROM/AROM (flex, ER, abd), avoid active IR and horizontal adduction until 10-12 wks, light UE resistance (avoid IR until wk 10); 12+ wks: begin active IR/adduction, full AROM, progressive strengthening, single-arm pec exercises, plyometrics when full strength achieved, sport-specific; RTS: doctor clearance, strength test, throwing program if applicable
Notes: Avoid active IR and horizontal adduction until 12 weeks; passive FF limited to 130° first 4 weeks; passive ER limited to 25° weeks 4-6; UE resistive exercises (avoiding IR) begin week 6-12; single-arm pec strengthening begins after 12 weeks; plyometrics only when aggressive resistance achieved with full strength; long recovery expected for return to contact/overhead sport

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair with Pectoralis Major Tendon Transfer (Open)
WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling at all times 0-6 weeks; no AROM of shoulder for first 6 weeks; no pendulum exercises weeks 1-4; PROM limited to 90° FF and neutral IR/ER in first 4 weeks; must elevate arm without scapular hiking before initiating isotonics; avoid isometric with pec in lengthened position; no lifting/overhead/repetitive shoulder motions
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): sling, no AROM, no pendulums wks 1-4, PROM (FF 90° then full wks 4-6, IR/ER neutral then 45° in scapular plane), AAROM at wk 5, peri-scapular exercises, sub-max deltoid/IR/ER isometrics at wk 5; Phase II (6-10 wks): discontinue sling, PROM to full, AAROM/AROM (wand), full AROM by wk 10, peri-scapular progress, isometrics (avoid pec in lengthened position); Phase III (10-14 wks): maintain full AROM, UBE, isotonic dumbbells, PNF diagonals, prone rowing/horizontal abd/"full can", submaximal plyometrics (chest pass, side throws, body blade); Phase IV (14+ wks): full AROM, progressive strengthening/power, advanced plyometrics, sport/work-specific
Notes: No pendulum exercises weeks 1-4 (unusual); no AROM for first 6 weeks; PROM limited to 90° FF and neutral IR/ER in first 4 weeks; avoid isometric with pec in lengthened position; submaximal 2-handed plyometrics begin Phase 3 (10-14 weeks); open rotator cuff repair combined with pec major tendon transfer for massive/irreparable RC tears; 4 phases

WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling at all times except HEP and PT for 6 weeks; sleep in sling; no cross-body or IR motion in Phase 2 (2-6 wks); no lifting >2 lbs through Phase 3; limit IR strengthening in Phase 3; dressings changed on 7th day (not 2nd day); PT begins within first week post-op, 2-3x/week through 12 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): sling, pendulums, passive supine FF to 130°, passive supine ER to 25°, peri-scapular exercises, deltoid isometrics, wrist/hand exercises; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): sling continues, PROM (FF 140°, ER 60°, abd 120°), AAROM FF to 90° with pulleys, no cross-body or IR; Phase 3 (6-12 wks): discontinue sling, PROM/AROM all directions, Theraband (FF, ext, ER), limit IR strengthening; Phase 4 (12+ wks): advance end-range ROM, restore scapulothoracic rhythm, increase resistive exercises, transition to HEP
Notes: Updated 2024 version; PT begins within first week, 2-3x/week through 12 weeks; dressings changed on day 7 (not day 2); no cross-body or IR motion in Phase 2; limit IR strengthening in Phase 3; 4 phases; discuss activity/sport restrictions with surgeon

WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling at all times except HEP and PT for 6 weeks; sleep in sling; no cross-body or IR motion in Phase 2 (2-6 wks); no lifting >2 lbs through Phase 3; limit IR strengthening in Phase 3; dressings changed on 7th day; PT begins within first week post-op, 2-3x/week through 12 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): sling, pendulums, passive supine FF to 130°, passive supine ER to 25°, peri-scapular exercises, deltoid isometrics, wrist/hand exercises; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): sling continues, PROM (FF 140°, ER 60°, abd 120°), AAROM FF to 90° with pulleys or cane, no cross-body or IR; Phase 3 (6-12 wks): discontinue sling, PROM/AROM all directions, Theraband (FF, ext, ER), limit IR strengthening; Phase 4 (12+ wks): advance end-range ROM, restore scapulothoracic rhythm, increase resistive exercises, transition to HEP
Notes: Updated 2024 version; PT begins within first week, 2-3x/week through 12 weeks; dressings changed on day 7; no cross-body or IR motion in Phase 2; limit IR strengthening in Phase 3; AAROM uses pulleys or cane in Phase 2; 4 phases; discuss activity/sport restrictions with surgeon

Surgery Type: Shoulder Lysis of Adhesions / Manipulation under Anesthesia (LOA/MUA)
WB Status: NWB surgical UE; no sling use after isolated LOA/MUA
Key Restrictions: NO sling use after isolated LOA/MUA procedure; PT must begin within days of surgery (ideally day 1-2); 3x/week therapy plus daily HEP; aggressive early ROM emphasis
Timeline / Phases: Day 1-2 weeks: NO sling, maximize PROM and AROM immediately (full ROM achieved during LOA/MUA), AAROM with canes and pulleys then progress, light RC and peri-scapular strengthening as tolerated by motion; 2+ weeks: restore full AROM, modalities as needed to assist motion, progressive RC and peri-scapular strengthening, continue HEP on non-PT days
Notes: Key distinction: NO sling use with isolated LOA/MUA (opposite of most shoulder procedures); PT begins day 1-2 post-op, 3x/week; aggressive early ROM to capitalize on intraoperative gains; 2-phase protocol; light strengthening progresses with ROM; modalities used to manage pain and assist motion gains; if combined with another procedure, refer to combined protocol

Surgery Type: LOA/MUA with Biceps Tenodesis and/or Small Rotator Cuff Repair
WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling determined by surgeon based on concomitant procedure: tenodesis = no sling or 4 weeks; RCR = sling 6 weeks; avoid resisted elbow flexion/forearm supination (tenodesis) initially; avoid IR/ER resistance (RCR) initially; no biceps loading until week 10; no sling beyond 6 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (Day 1-2 wks): sling per surgeon/procedure, AAROM with cane/pulleys to restore full PROM, avoid active elbow flex/supination (tenodesis) or IR/ER resistance (RCR), peri-scapular strengthening; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): maintain full PROM, gentle progressive AROM, shoulder isometrics to isotonics, peri-scapular exercises, discontinue sling at 4 or 6 wks per surgeon; Phase 3 (7-12 wks): restore full AROM/PROM, light resistive tubing/bands, prone I's/Y's/T's, resistive biceps loading at wk 10; Phase 4 (12+ wks): dynamic stabilization, progressive resistance (0.5 kg/10 days), NM control with D1/D2 patterns
Notes: Sling use depends on concomitant procedure (tenodesis vs RCR); early aggressive ROM like isolated LOA/MUA but with repair-specific restrictions layered on; avoid resistive biceps loading until week 10; resistance progression 0.5 kg/10 days; NM control with D1/D2 in Phase 4; 4 phases

WB Status: NWB surgical UE
Key Restrictions: Sling 3-4 weeks (surgeon-determined); sleep in sling first 3 weeks; avoid resisted elbow flexion/forearm supination for 6 weeks; no active ER/extension/abduction for 4-6 weeks; no biceps loading until week 10; return to running/biking/Stairmaster/golf (proper kinematics) permitted after week 8
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): sling 3-4 wks, PROM (pendulums, flex/scaption 90°, ER 40°, IR 45°), scapulothoracic AROM, scapular stabilization/isometrics, gripping; Phase 2 (3-6 wks): wean sling at surgeon discretion, PROM progressing (flex/scaption 145°, ER 50°, IR 60°), AROM supination (no resistance), scapulothoracic isometrics; Phase 3 (7-12 wks): full AROM, resisted IR/ER at 30-90° abd, cautious biceps curl/supination/pronation, prone scapular program, push-up plus, rhythmic stabilization, D1/D2; Phase 4 (12+ wks): plyometrics (bilateral then unilateral), sport/throwing progression; RTS: pain-free control with sport-specific patterns, kinematic transfer hip/core to shoulder
Notes: Sling shorter (3-4 weeks) than labral repairs; no active ER/ext/abd for 4-6 weeks; no biceps loading until week 10; running/biking/golf permitted after week 8 (not shoulder-specific); 4 phases; very similar biceps restrictions to SLAP protocol; prone scapular program begins week 6; plyometrics begin Phase 4 (12+ weeks)

WB Status: NWB/minimal UE (1-2 lbs) weeks 2-6; up to 10 lbs weeks 6-12
Key Restrictions: Sling when awake and moving weeks 0-2; sling for comfort weeks 2-6 (remove several times daily for elbow/wrist stretching); discontinue sling at 6 weeks; use pain as guide throughout; no active shoulder ROM until week 6
Timeline / Phases: 0-2 wks: sling when awake, elbow/wrist stretching, comfortable sleep positioning; 2-6 wks: 1-2 lb UE WB, sling for comfort, pendulums, AAROM/PROM (forward elevation, supported rotation); 6-12 wks: up to 10 lb WB, discontinue sling, outpatient PT, active ROM (wall walks, elevation, abduction), shoulder isometrics (ext/ER/IR/add/abd), isotonic IR/ER with <5 lbs
Notes: UVA Orthopaedic Trauma; HEP-based protocol (3 sessions/day, 10 reps); pain used as primary guide for progression; outpatient PT begins at 6 weeks; active shoulder ROM and strengthening delayed until 6 weeks to protect fracture healing

WB Status: Minimal UE WB (1-2 lbs) weeks 2-4; up to 5 lbs weeks 4-6
Key Restrictions: Sling for comfort weeks 2-4; discontinue sling at 4-6 weeks; Aspirin 325 mg daily for DVT prophylaxis; PROM/AAROM only weeks 2-4; active ROM and progressive strengthening from weeks 4-6
Timeline / Phases: 2-4 wks: 1-2 lb WB, sling for comfort, Aspirin 325 mg, elbow/wrist stretching, pendulums, AAROM/PROM (forward elevation, supported rotation); 4-6 wks: up to 5 lb WB, discontinue sling, continue Aspirin, active ROM (wall walks, elevation, abduction — 3 reps only), shoulder isometrics (ext/ER/IR/add/abd), isotonic IR/ER with <5 lbs
Notes: UVA Orthopaedic Trauma ORIF protocol; Aspirin 325 mg daily for DVT; active ROM begins earlier (wks 4-6) than non-op (wks 6-12); WB limit 5 lbs at 4-6 weeks (vs. 10 lbs at 6-12 weeks for non-op); 2-phase post-op protocol; same exercise program as non-op from phase 2 onward

WB Status: NWB/minimal UE (1-2 lbs) weeks 2-6; up to 10 lbs weeks 6-12
Key Restrictions: Sling when awake and moving weeks 0-2; sling for comfort weeks 2-6 (remove several times daily for elbow/wrist stretching); discontinue sling at 6 weeks; use pain as guide throughout; no active shoulder ROM until week 6
Timeline / Phases: 0-2 wks: sling when awake, elbow/wrist stretching, comfortable sleep positioning; 2-6 wks: 1-2 lb UE WB, sling for comfort, pendulums, AAROM/PROM (forward elevation, supported rotation); 6-12 wks: up to 10 lb WB, discontinue sling, outpatient PT, active ROM (wall walks, elevation, abduction), shoulder isometrics (ext/ER/IR/add/abd), isotonic IR/ER with <5 lbs
Notes: UVA Orthopaedic Trauma; HEP-based protocol (3 sessions/day, 10 reps); pain as primary guide; outpatient PT begins at 6 weeks; exercise program identical to UVA clavicle non-op protocol; active ROM and strengthening delayed until 6 weeks to protect fracture healing

WB Status: Minimal UE WB (1-2 lbs) weeks 2-6; WBAT weeks 6-12 unless instructed otherwise by physician
Key Restrictions: Sling for comfort weeks 2-6; discontinue sling at 6 weeks; Aspirin 325 mg daily (discontinue at 6-12 weeks); PROM/AAROM only weeks 2-6; active ROM and progressive strengthening from week 6; longer protection phase than clavicle ORIF
Timeline / Phases: 2-6 wks: 1-2 lb WB, sling for comfort, Aspirin 325 mg, elbow/wrist stretching, pendulums, AAROM/PROM (forward elevation, supported rotation); 6-12 wks: WBAT (per physician), discontinue sling and Aspirin, outpatient PT, active ROM (wall walks, elevation, abduction — 3 reps only), shoulder isometrics (ext/ER/IR/add/abd), isotonic IR/ER with <5 lbs
Notes: UVA Orthopaedic Trauma; Aspirin 325 mg discontinued at 6-12 weeks (vs. ongoing in clavicle ORIF); WBAT from week 6 (vs. 5-lb limit for clavicle ORIF); 2-phase protocol; PROM/AAROM phase lasts 4 weeks (vs. 2 weeks for clavicle ORIF) reflecting greater surgical complexity; outpatient PT from week 6

WB Status: 50% WB with crutches (foot flat) for 4 weeks; wean crutches starting week 4; FWB when comfortable with no gait deviations
Key Restrictions: Hip flexion <90° Phase 1; hip flexion <105°, ER <20° Phase 2; naproxen 500 mg twice daily x30 days to prevent heterotopic ossification; aspirin/DVT prophylaxis x2 weeks; no driving 4-6 weeks; ice hip 20-30 min 5-6x/day; sleep with pillow between knees
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): 50% WB foot-flat, hip flex <90°, no excessive IR/ER, stationary bike high seat no resistance, quad/glute/HS sets, adductor/abductor isometrics, double-leg bridges, heel slides; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): wean crutches from wk 4, hip flex <105°, ER <20°, clam shells, single-leg bridges, leg press, mini squats, 4-way hip, aqua therapy once portals healed; Phase 3 (6-12 wks): FWB, full ROM symmetry, clam shells with band, side-lying planks, physioball HS, side-stepping with resistance, lunges, NM control/single-leg balance, elliptical/treadmill; Phase 4 (12-16 wks): full ROM, low-impact plyometrics, swimming, sport-specific agility, running progression, traditional weight training
Notes: Naproxen 500 mg 2x daily x30 days post-op to prevent heterotopic ossification (do not combine with other NSAIDs); 50% WB maintained foot-flat to reduce hip joint force; crutch weaning begins week 4; RTS criteria: full pain-free ROM symmetric to opposite side, symmetric hip strength, stable pelvis, sport-specific drills at full speed without pain; 4 phases

WB Status: 25% WB weeks 0-2; 50% WB weeks 2-6; WBAT by week 8
Key Restrictions: Brace as indicated per tear severity; no passive hip adduction, ER, or IR Phase 1; no active hip abduction or IR Phase 1; hip flexion limited to 90° Phase 1; active hip abduction and IR delayed until Phase 3 (6-12 weeks); resisted abduction and IR not until Phase 4 (12+ weeks)
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): 25% WB, brace, PROM (flex 90°, abd as tolerated, ext neutral), no passive add/ER/IR, no active abd/IR, upright stationary bike non-op leg only, hip isometrics ext/add, quad/HS sets, lower abdominal activation; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): 50% WB, AAROM abd/IR, PROM ER/IR/add to neutral, AROM hip flexion, core stabilization, quad/HS isotonics, bridges, prone hip extension, hip flexor stretching; Phase 3 (6-12 wks): WBAT by wk 8, full PROM, start active abd/IR, eccentric step-downs, lateral walks, balance bilateral, stretching (hip flexor/adductor/glutes/piriformis/ITB/TFL); Phase 4 (12+ wks): full ROM, resisted abd/IR, gradual abductor/adductor strengthening, lunges, plyometrics, single-leg balance progression
Notes: 4 phases; brace based on tear severity; upright stationary bike with no resistance, pushing pedal with non-op leg only in Phase 1; active hip abduction and IR not until Phase 3; resisted abduction and IR not until Phase 4; comprehensive stretching (hip flexor, adductor, glutes, piriformis, ITB, TFL) from Phase 3; gait normalization emphasis in Phase 4

WB Status: TTWB weeks 0-2; 50% WB weeks 2-6; wean crutches over 2 weeks in Phase 3 (weeks 6-8); WBAT thereafter
Key Restrictions: Knee brace locked at 30° extension for ambulation x6 weeks (may unlock for therapy); no active knee flexion or hip extension Phase 1; protect against simultaneous knee flexion and hip extension throughout all phases; passive knee ROM performed in prone with hip in full extension; walk-to-jog progression at 16 weeks; running/agility at 20 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase 1 (0-2 wks): TTWB, brace locked 30°, no active knee flex or hip ext, passive hip flex to 60° (knee flexed), passive knee ROM (no hip flexion during knee extension), quad sets, ankle pumps, abdominal isometrics; Phase 2 (2-6 wks): 50% WB, brace locked 30° ambulation (unlock okay), prone passive knee ROM in full hip extension, AAROM knee flex/hip ext, hip flex to 90°, AAROM hip abduction, calf raises, side-lying hip abduction, upper body training; Phase 3 (6-12 wks): wean crutches, wean brace, gradual active knee flexion, AAROM to full active hip extension, hip abd/add, stationary bike high seat, antigravity HS curls, SLR, bridges, clam shells, half squats; Phase 4 (12+ wks): full ROM, closed chain bilateral to single leg, gait normalization, walk-to-jog at 16 wks, running/speed/agility at 20 wks
Notes: Critical: protect against simultaneous knee flexion and hip extension throughout all phases — this is the primary repair protection; knee brace locked 30° extension x6 weeks for ambulation; passive knee ROM in prone with hip in full extension (never combined with hip flexion); upper body strength training permitted Phase 2; walk-to-jog at 16 weeks; progressive running and agility at 20 weeks; 4 phases

WB Status: NWB weeks 0-2; PWB with crutches progressing to FWB by weeks 3-4; FWB in boot weeks 4-8; boot discontinued after 8+ weeks (once walking without heel lift for 1 week)
Key Restrictions: Equinus cast 15-20° plantarflexion weeks 0-2 (posterior plaster splint if swelling severe); boot with 1" heel lift (4 x 1/4" lifts) weeks 2-4; remove one 1/4" lift per week with goal of no lift at 8 weeks; DF stretching and resisted plantarflexion not until week 6; boot discontinued only after walking 1 full week without heel lift
Timeline / Phases: Wks 0-2: equinus cast 15-20° PF (posterior plaster splint if severe swelling), NWB; Wks 2-4: boot with 1" heel lift (4 x 1/4" lifts), PWB with crutches progressing to FWB by wks 3-4, AROM plantarflexion (unrestricted) and dorsiflexion to neutral in boot; Wks 4-8: FWB in boot, remove 1/4" heel lift each week (goal: no lift at wk 8), DF stretching from wk 6, resisted plantarflexion from wk 6; Wks 8-12: discontinue boot after 1 week of walking without lift, progressive PT-guided strengthening/ROM/proprioception
Notes: Graduated heel lift removal (1/4" per week) is key: 1" total at week 2, none by week 8; active plantarflexion unrestricted in boot from week 2; DF to neutral permitted from week 2 but DF stretching and resisted PF not until week 6; boot discontinued once patient walks without any heel lift for 1 week; strengthening and proprioception training begin weeks 8-12 under PT guidance

WB Status: PWB: 20 lb foot flat x 2 wks; hip plications: PWB 4-6 wks; microfracture: PWB 6 wks; FWB by ~6 wks standard cases

WB Status: NWB x 2-4 wks (younger/athletic, lesion <1 cm, central/posterior talus); NWB up to 6 wks (anterior lesion, smokers, high BMI, older age, lesion >1 cm); progress to WBAT in normal shoe with lace-up ankle brace at wks 6-12

Key Restrictions: QTG at higher risk for flexion loss and scar tissue — early mobility critical; brace unlocked within 1 wk; Thomas test position + soft tissue work for ROM; LET may be added; no impact until Biodex <30% and Y balance >95%; ROOT repair: TDWB locked brace x6 wks
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks: PWB to WBAT, early ROM priority) -> Phase II (4-6 wks+: normalize gait, CKC) -> Phase III (8-12 wks+: progressive strength, RFD, no impact) -> Phase IV/V (cutting/pivoting, RTS at 9+ months)
Notes: QTG: prioritize early ROM/mobility more than other grafts to prevent stiffness and scar tissue; LET may be added; NMES and BFR included; Spectrum class; surgeon-specific meniscal precautions (Baer/Cotter/Day/Smith/Spiker vs. Scerpella/Sheean); RTS at 9+ months; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgery Type: ACL Reconstruction — Hamstring Graft, No Hardware (Older Adolescent)
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: PWB + locked brace wks 0-2; WBAT + 1-2 crutches + locked brace wks 3-4; WBAT +/- crutches + unlocked brace wks 5-6
Key Restrictions: Longer brace/crutch use than adult; ROM 0-90 x 4 weeks; avoid aggressive hamstring sets early (donor site); ROOT repair: TDWB locked brace x6 wks; meniscal precautions surgeon-specific (Baer/Walczak/Spiker vs. Scerpella)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks: PWB to WBAT, locked brace, ROM 0-90) -> Phase II (6-8 wks: normalize gait, CKC) -> Phase III (12-16 wks: running + impact prep; Biodex <25%) -> Phase IV (18-22 wks: multi-planar high velocity; Biodex <15%) -> Phase V (28-36 wks: RTS)
Notes: Updated 1/2018; over-the-top or soft-tissue tunnel without hardware across growth plates (Tanner Stage 2-3; males ~13-16, females ~12-14); fixation less strong than adult; RTS criteria: <15% Biodex, force plate, hop tests; 5-phase protocol; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: TDWB wks 1-2; PWB wks 3-6; WBAT after ~7 weeks
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension x 6 wks; ROM 0-60 wks 1-2 and 0-90 wks 3-6 (strict — protects periosteal stitch on tibia); TDWB x 2 wks; no impact until Phase III; meniscal repair: TDWB x6 wks, same ROM limits
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-8 wks: TDWB to PWB, brace locked x6 wks, strict ROM) -> Phase II (7-8 wks+: normalize gait, CKC) -> Phase III (14-16 wks+: low-amp running/impact; Biodex <25%) -> Phase IV (22-26 wks: multi-planar; Biodex <15%) -> Phase V (9-10 months: RTS, Biodex <10-15%)
Notes: Updated 1/2018; all-extraphyseal IT band reconstruction — no bone tunnels, no hardware (Tanner Stage 1-2; males <12, females <11); ROM restrictions imperative to protect periosteal tibial stitch; 5-phase protocol; RTS 9-10 months; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: PWB progressing to WBAT; brace locked in extension during all WB/walking x 6 weeks
Key Restrictions: Brace locked x 6 wks for all WB; ROM 0-90 x 4 wks; avoid aggressive hamstring activities early (donor site); fixation weaker than adult; no impact until Y balance >95% LSI and Biodex <30%; ROOT repair: TDWB locked x6 wks
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks: PWB to WBAT, locked brace x6 wks, ROM 0-90) -> Phase II (6+ wks: normalize gait, unlock brace, CKC) -> Phase III (11-12 wks+: strength, RFD, no impact; Y balance >95%, Biodex <30%) -> Phase IV/V (RTS at 9+ months)
Notes: Over-the-top technique (no femoral tunnel) for adolescents with partially closed growth plates; fixation less strong than adult; 9-12 months PT; NMES and BFR included; Spectrum class; surgeon-specific meniscal precautions (Baer/Cotter/Day/Smith/Spiker vs. Scerpella/Sheean); UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgery Type: Tibial Spine Avulsion Fracture Surgical Fixation
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: TDWB wks 1-2; PWB (max 50% BW) wks 3-6; WBAT after 6 weeks
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension x 6 wks; ROM graded in 30° increments: 0-30° wks 1-2, 30-60° wks 3-4, 60-90° wks 5-6 (imperative to protect periosteal stitch); meniscal repair: NWB flexion x4 wks; ROOT repair: TDWB locked x6 wks, NWB ROM 0-90°
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks: TDWB to PWB, locked brace, graded ROM) -> Phase II (6+ wks: normalize gait, CKC) -> Phase III (12-14 wks: running/impact; Biodex <25%) -> Phase IV (20-24 wks: multi-planar, Biodex <20%) -> Phase V (26-28 wks: cutting/pivoting, RTS; Biodex <10%)
Notes: 5-phase protocol; ROM restriction imperative for periosteal stitch; NMES for quad activation; Type I (non-displaced) managed non-operatively with straight-leg brace x6 wks then rehab; Types II-III require surgical fixation (suture/screw arthroscopic); pediatric patients may stay overnight at American Family Children's Hospital; 6-12 months total PT; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgery Type: Chronic Exertional Compartment Syndrome (CECS) Release (Open Fasciotomy)
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: NWB x 2 wks; WBAT in walking boot wk 3
Key Restrictions: NWB x 2 wks (Dr. Scerpella: splint + NWB first 4-7 days); no impact/running/jumping Phase I; no eccentric loading Phase II; no running until 8 wks; avoid friction on scar; swelling must not increase >0.5 cm per session
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks: NWB to WBAT boot, swelling management, PROM) -> Phase II (3-4 wks+: normalize gait, scar massage, bike/swim if healed) -> Phase III (6-8 wks+: plyometrics at 7 wks, light jogging at 8 wks) -> Phase IV (8-12 wks+: sport-specific; FAAM 9-pt sport improvement)
Notes: Updated 2/2018; FAAM (ADL + sport subscales) administered at baseline and discharge; within 90% PF/DF strength for RTS; video gait/biomechanical analysis in Phase IV; protective padding for collision sports over scar; 95% of CECS cases occur in lower leg; 85-95% bilateral symptoms; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: WBAT with axillary crutches initially; progress to independent ambulation as tolerated
Key Restrictions: Axillary crutches initially for gait; avoid impact x 4-6 wks if articular cartilage debrided; post-activity soreness must resolve within 24 hrs; avoid post-activity swelling
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2-3 wks: WBAT with crutches, restore ROM, normalize gait, eliminate swelling) -> Phase II (after Phase I: SLS control, functional movements, non-impact strengthening) -> Phase III (after Phase II: impact control, multi-planar, RTS)
Notes: Updated 5/2019; 3-phase criterion-based protocol; covers partial meniscectomy, articular cartilage debridement, fat pad impingement, plica removal; articular cartilage debridement adds 4-6 wk impact restriction; RTS: dynamic neuromuscular control multi-plane without pain/swelling; no structured RTS testing described; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: Activity restriction; brace or crutches as needed; goal is protected loading to restore blood flow and allow bone healing
Key Restrictions: Avoid running, jumping, high-impact activities; bracing or crutches PRN; activity restriction duration 6 months to 1 year; imaging follow-up (X-ray/MRI) to confirm healing; surgery indicated if non-op fails, lesion unstable, or growth plates closed
Timeline / Phases: Non-operative track: activity modification and protected loading -> progressive return to impact guided by imaging and symptom resolution; no formal phase structure for non-operative management
Notes: Most common in ages 10-15; more common in boys; juvenile OCD (open growth plates) has better healing prognosis; non-op management can achieve complete healing; surgical option if OCD does not heal or lesion is unstable; document also contains embedded surgical phase protocol; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: TDWB with crutches x 8 wks; then WBAT
Key Restrictions: TDWB x 8 wks; hinged or unloader brace; Phase I: high volume/low load (2-3x/day, high reps, minimal resistance); CKC squat limited to 0-60° ROM in Phase II; Biodex <25% deficit before impact
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-8 wks: TDWB, brace, quad/NWB exercises, high-volume low-load) -> Phase II (8-12 wks: normalize gait, CKC 0-60°, bike/elliptical) -> Phase III (12-18 wks: low-amp impact at ~14-15 wks; Biodex <20%) -> Phase IV (18-24 wks: multi-planar, RTS)
Notes: Copyright 2018; 4-phase protocol; surgical options: arthroscopic drilling (to improve blood supply), fragment fixation (screws/nails/sutures), bone graft for large lesions; Biodex <25% to initiate impact; RTS with surgeon clearance; imaging follow-up post-op; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation & Sports Medicine Physician Group
WB Status: NWB x 6 wks; 25-50% BW wk 7; 50-100% BW wk 8; brace locked for all WB
Key Restrictions: NWB x 6 wks; brace locked for all WB activities; no open chain hamstring strengthening (through Phase III); no hamstring stretching; no bike (Phase II); PROM only with posterior support for PCL; no forced hyperextension; impact requires >=80% strength (SL press or Biodex)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-8 wks: NWB to PWB, locked brace, PROM, quad activation) -> Phase II (8+ wks: normalize gait, unlock brace wks 8-12, CKC <=60°) -> Phase III (~16 wks: SL control, quad OKC/CKC, no hamstring OKC) -> Phase IV (24-28 wks: impact >=80% strength, multi-planar, RTS)
Notes: Updated 2/2018; surgery best within 3 weeks post-injury after swelling reduction; covers ACL/PCL/MCL/LCL combinations (autograft or allograft); Biodex testing at 6 and 9 months; impact initiation at >=80% SL press or Biodex; no hamstring OKC through Phase III to protect PCL repair; 4-phase protocol; UW Health Sports Medicine, Madison WI

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS, PES; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: NWB until post-op surgeon visit; then TDWB in boot or cast
Key Restrictions: No inversion/eversion ROM for 6 weeks; no jumping/hopping until Phase IV (~14 weeks); wean to ankle stabilizing orthosis (ASO)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): NWB/TDWB, no ROM; Phase II (6-10 wks): progressive WB, DF/PF ROM, no inversion/eversion; Phase III (10-14 wks): full ROM, full peroneal strength, normal gait; Phase IV (14-16+ wks): sport-specific prep, clearance from surgeon and PT/ATC
Notes: Brostrom and modified Brostrom-Evans technique. Allograft reconstruction described for severe instability. Criterion-based progression. Developed by Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS, PES.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: TF: axillary crutches, PWB ≤30% BW, brace 0-45° (Phase I); PF: PWB ≤50% BW, locked extension (Phase I); WBAT Phase III
Key Restrictions: No impact until Phase IV (3-6 months); TF closed chain exercises limited to <50° knee flex in Phase III; TF and PF graft variants have distinct WB/ROM restrictions; some patients advised against lifetime impact sport
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 wks): PWB, ROM 0-90° TF/0-60° PF; Phase II (4-6 wks): PWB ≤60%/WBAT, ROM 0-125°; Phase III (7-12 wks): WBAT, full ROM, CKC <50°; Phase IV (3-6 mo): non-impact sport (LSI ≥85%); Phase V (9-16 mo): impact and RTS (LSI ≥90%, force plate)
Notes: 5-phase criterion-based protocol. TF and PF graft variants have distinct WB/ROM restrictions. Phase V may not apply if patient is advised to minimize lifetime impact sports. Copyright 2019, updated 08/2025.

WB Status: WBAT with crutches; post-op brace locked in extension initially
Key Restrictions: Post-op brace locked in extension; avoid rotational knee movements; no impact until Phase III (12+ weeks); NMES recommended for quad strengthening throughout Phase I-II
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): crutches + locked brace, ROM goal 90°, normalize gait; Phase II (6-12 wks): CKC strength, LSI >90% single leg press/Y-Balance, quad deficit <30%; Phase III (12-16 wks): impact drills, return-to-run; Phase IV (20+ wks): RTS (quad deficit <10%, hop test LSI >90%)
Notes: Covers both repair and reconstruction. NMES dosing appendix with Spector et al. algorithm included. Modifications for associated patellar/trochlear chondroplasty noted. RTS requires surgeon and PT/ATC clearance plus functional testing.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: Progressive WB with locked knee brace for 4 weeks; crutch weaning as tolerated
Key Restrictions: Locked knee brace for all WB for 4 weeks; no knee flexion >90° for 4 weeks; no forced WB flexion >60°; no impact until Phase III (~3 months); physician may extend Phase I to 6 weeks for large repairs
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks): locked brace, ROM <90°; Phase II (4 wks+): normalize gait, no WB >60° flexion, no impact; Phase III (3 mo+): impact training, RTS (dynamic neuromuscular control without pain/swelling)
Notes: Criterion-based. Size and location of tear affects rate of progression. Physician may extend Phase I to 6 weeks for large repairs. Developed by Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS.

Surgery Type: Meniscal Root and Complex Tear Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: TDWB with crutches for 8 weeks; post-op extension brace for 6 weeks
Key Restrictions: TDWB for 8 weeks; post-op extension brace for 6 weeks; ROM goal 0-90° in Phase I; no WB >60° flexion in Phase II; no impact until Phase III (12-16 weeks)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-8 wks): TDWB, ROM 0-90°, extension brace; Phase II (8+ wks): normalize gait, progressive loading, no impact; Phase III (12-16 wks): agility, landing mechanics, running; Phase IV (20-24 wks): RTS (<15% deficit on Biodex, force plate, and hop tests)
Notes: More restrictive than standard meniscal repair. Covers posterior root tears (transtibial tunnel or suture anchor repair) and complex tears. 4-phase criterion-based protocol. RTS requires surgeon/PT clearance plus functional testing.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: FC lesion: NWB 0-2 wks → TDWB 3-4 wks → WBAT 5-6 wks; PF lesion: TDWB 0-2 wks → WBAT 3-6 wks (Scerpella: WBAT 0-6 wks)
Key Restrictions: Axillary crutches 6 weeks; PF lesions: locked knee brace 6 weeks; ROM exercises 4-8 hrs/day in Phase I (CPM); no impact until 12 weeks; WB varies by lesion location and surgeon
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): NWB→WBAT per protocol, ROM exercises 4-8 hrs/day, CPM; Phase II (6+ wks): normalize gait, no impact until 12 wks; Phase III (3 mo+): impact control, RTS (dynamic neuromuscular control without pain/swelling)
Notes: WB varies by lesion location (femoral condyle vs. patellofemoral) and by surgeon (Baer/Day/Spiker/Walczak vs. Scerpella for PF lesions). CPM and frequent ROM exercises emphasized in Phase I. Return to impact sport may require 9-16 months.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry, PT, LAT, CSCS; UW Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: PWB 20-25% weeks 1-6; locked extension brace 0-6 weeks
Key Restrictions: PWB 20-25% for 6 weeks; locked extension brace 6 weeks; ROM exercises 4-6 hrs/day Phase I; ROM progression: 0-90° wks 1-2, 0-110° wks 3-4, 0-125° wks 5-6; CKC <60° knee flex Phase II; no impact until Phase III (~4 months)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): 20-25% PWB, locked brace, ROM 4-6 hrs/day; Phase II (7-12 wks): progressive WBAT, CKC <60°, no impact until 12 wks; Phase III (4 mo+): functional strengthening, non-impact cardio; Phase IV (6 mo+): impact, RTS (moderate impact 8 mo, high impact 10 mo)
Notes: OATS restores native hyaline cartilage vs. fibrocartilage from microfracture. Single large plug or multiple smaller plugs. Frequent ROM exercises (4-6 hrs/day) critical in Phase I. Gudas et al.: 93% OATS vs. 52% microfracture returned to prior sport level. Marc Sherry, PT, LAT, CSCS.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: Scerpella/Day/Spiker: NWB or TDWB for 6 weeks; Baer: WBAT without pain; brace locked in extension for gait and ADLs
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension for all gait/ADLs; no driving Phase I; CKC 0-30° progressing to 0-60° Phase II; no CKC >90° Phase III; avoid post-activity swelling
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): locked brace, surgeon-specific WB, ROM 0-90°; Phase II (6-10 wks): progressive WB, short arc CKC, balance; Phase III (10 wks+): full ROM, CKC up to 90°, quadriceps/hip/core; Phase IV (14-16 wks+): impact, RTS (<15% deficit Biodex, force plate, hop tests)
Notes: Covers distal realignment (tibial tubercle osteotomy), MPFL repair/reconstruction, and lateral retinacular release. WB restrictions vary significantly by surgeon. 4-phase criterion-based protocol with functional testing for RTS clearance.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: WBAT with brace locked in extension and axillary crutches; brace weaned weeks 4-6 as leg control improves
Key Restrictions: No open chain hamstring strengthening or hamstring stretching through Phase II; no bike Phase I-II; brace locked for all WB Phase I; ROM 0-90° Phase I; CKC <70° knee flex Phase II; no forced hyperflexion
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks): WBAT + locked brace, ROM 0-90°, no hamstring work; Phase II (5-12 wks): normalize gait, ROM to 120°, CKC <70°, no hamstring; Phase III (12-16 wks): agility, open/closed chain quads, light impact; Phase IV (24+ wks): RTS (dynamic neuromuscular control without instability/pain/swelling)
Notes: No open chain hamstring exercises until Phase III — critical restriction as hamstrings stress the PCL graft via posterior tibial translation. Brace weaned weeks 4-6. 4-phase criterion-based protocol. Copyright 2018.

Surgery Type: Patellar Tendon and Quadriceps Tendon Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: WBAT with dial brace locked in extension and axillary crutches
Key Restrictions: Dial brace locked in extension at all times except during rehab exercises; no active knee extension weeks 3-6; WB knee flexion <70° for 12 weeks; avoid forceful eccentrics; no impact until Phase V (~4 months)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): WBAT + locked brace; Phase II (2-6 wks): normalize gait, ROM 0-90° (no active quad extension); Phase III (6-12 wks): brace open 30-40°, active quad contractions, CKC 0-40°; Phase IV (12 wks+): full ROM, single leg balance, CKC to 70°; Phase V (4 mo+): impact, RTS (surgeon and PT clearance)
Notes: Covers both patellar tendon and quadriceps tendon repairs. Relaxing suture placed at surgery for additional protection. No active knee extension until Phase III. ROM limits may be modified by surgeon based on repair integrity. 5-phase criterion-based protocol.

Surgery Type: Anterior Shoulder Reconstruction with Arthroscopic Bankart Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 3-4 weeks; no shoulder ER with abduction for 6 weeks; no swimming at any phase; no running/jumping until full rotator cuff strength
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): sling 3-4 wks, ER to neutral, isometrics from week 3; Phase II (6-11 wks): full ROM, RC strength at 45° abd; Phase III (10-11 wks+): 5/5 RC at 90° abd, no sports; Phase IV (15 wks+): high velocity, plyometrics; Phase V (20 wks+): sport-specific program, RTS (surgeon and PT clearance)
Notes: Arthroscopic Bankart repair for anterior shoulder instability. 5-phase criterion-based protocol. No swimming at any phase due to distractive forces. Axillary nerve hypersensitivity common post-op. See also 2025 updated version from same institution. Copyright 2018.

Surgery Type: Anterior Shoulder Reconstruction with Arthroscopic Bankart Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 3-4 weeks; no shoulder ER with abduction for 6 weeks; Phase III requires ER ≥90° at 90° ABD and <10% LSI on strength and ASH tests; no swimming until Phase V
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): sling 3-4 wks, ER to neutral, isometrics week 3; Phase II (6-11 wks): full ROM, RC strength at 45°; Phase III (10-11 wks+): ER ≥90° at 90° ABD, <10% LSI strength and ASH; Phase IV (15 wks+): plyometrics, sport biomechanics; Phase V (20 wks+): RTS (overhead: 100% strength Biodex/ASH/grip; contact: + CKCUET)
Notes: Updated 2025 version with additional Phase III strength criteria (ASH testing, LSI <10%) and sport-specific RTS criteria distinguishing overhead vs. contact athletes (contact requires CKCUET). See also 2018 version from same institution. Copyright 2025.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 6 weeks (more restrictive than Bankart); no shoulder IR past neutral weeks 1-6; no IR with abduction weeks 1-8; ROM progresses by week in Phase I; ER in ABD limited to ~60° in Phase II; no swimming until Phase V
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): sling 6 wks, week-by-week ROM limits (no IR past neutral), isometrics from day 10; Phase II (6-12 wks): full ROM except ER in ABD ~60°, RC at 45°; Phase III (12 wks+): 5/5 RC at 90°, ER in ABD to 90°; Phase IV (15 wks+): high velocity, plyometrics; Phase V (20 wks+): sport-specific, RTS
Notes: For multi-directional shoulder instability. More restrictive than Bankart protocol: sling 6 weeks, no IR past neutral for 6 weeks, no IR with ABD for 8 weeks, ER in ABD limited to ~60° through Phase II. Week-by-week ROM progression in Phase I. Copyright 2018.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Medicine (surgeon-specific: Drs. Scerpella, Day, Cotter, Baer, Grogan)
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 1-2 weeks (surgeon-specific); minimize biceps tension 4-12 weeks (varies by surgeon); no resisted forearm supination or elbow flexion; 5 lb lifting restriction (Baer: 4 wks, Grogan: 8-12 wks); avoid extremes of shoulder extension and horizontal abduction
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4-6 wks): sling per surgeon, sub-max isometrics, no biceps loading; Phase II (6-8 wks): full AROM, RC strengthening; Phase III (8-12 wks): 5/5 RC at 90° abd, no sports; Phase IV (12 wks+): sport-specific strengthening, throwing program; Phase V (20 wks+): RTS (surgeon and PT clearance)
Notes: Surgeon-specific precautions for Drs. Scerpella/Day/Cotter/Baer/Grogan with varying sling duration and biceps loading restrictions. 5-phase protocol. May be done concurrently with rotator cuff surgery. Publication date not listed in document.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder/clavicle surgery — no WB through surgical arm)
Key Restrictions: Sling while sleeping 4 weeks; continuous sling weeks 0-2; no WB through surgical arm; no resisted IR or extension; cross body adduction restricted until 6 weeks; 5 lb lifting restriction until 12 weeks; Phase I ROM limits by week (90°/120° FF, 20°/30° ER)
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks): sling, strict ROM limits by week, no cross body adduction; Phase II (4-10 wks): gradual ROM to near full, gentle isometrics; Phase III (10-16 wks): full ROM by 12 wks, progressive strengthening, 80% strength; Phase IV (14-16+ wks): overhead strengthening, throwing program, RTS (<10% deficit UW UE progressive testing)
Notes: Covers midshaft, distal, and proximal clavicle ORIF. ROM restrictions specified week-by-week in Phase I. Neurocognitive strategies (dual task, visual scanning, cognitive challenges) recommended in Phase IV. RTS: <10% side-to-side deficit on UW Upper Extremity progressive testing. Copyright 2024.

Surgery Type: Distal Tibial Allograft (DTA) Anterior Shoulder Stabilization
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 4 weeks Phase I (no AROM); sling 6-8 weeks total; no ER >20° in neutral until Phase III; flexion/ABD limited to 135° Phase II; very gradual IR due to subscapularis split; if remplissage: limit eccentric infraspinatus strengthening
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4 wks): sling, no AROM, gentle Codman's only; Phase II (4-8 wks): sling 6-8 wks, isometrics, AAROM, flexion/ABD to 135°; Phase III (8-12+ wks): progressive AROM, ROM restrictions 8-12 wks; Phase IV (16-18 wks): 5/5 RC at 90°, no sports; Phase V (24 wks): plyometrics, sport biomechanics; Phase VI (30-32 wks): sport-specific RTS (overhead: 100% Biodex/ASH/grip; contact: +CKCUET)
Notes: 6-phase protocol for anterior glenoid bone loss treated with distal tibial allograft. More restrictive than arthroscopic Bankart: sling 6-8 wks, RTS at 30-32 weeks. Optional remplissage for Hill-Sachs lesion (limits infraspinatus eccentric loading). Copyright 2025.

Surgery Type: Posterior Shoulder Reconstruction with or without Labral Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS, PES; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 6 weeks; no shoulder IR past neutral for 6 weeks; no IR with abduction for 8 weeks; avoid closed chain exercises Phase II (posterior force stress); no swimming or running until Phase IV
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 wks): sling, ER to 40° in neutral, isometrics week 3; Phase II (3-8 wks): full ROM except IR, sling until 6 wks, no closed chain; Phase III (8-12 wks): 5/5 RC at 90°, normalize IR gradually; Phase IV (12+ wks): high velocity, sport biomechanics; Phase V (18+ wks): sport-specific, RTS (surgeon and PT clearance)
Notes: Restricts IR (not ER) to protect posterior repair — opposite of anterior instability protocols. No closed chain exercises in Phase II due to posterior force stress. Capsulolabral repair with/without labral debridement. Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS, PES. Copyright 2017.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling 4-6 weeks; no active elbow flexion for 3 weeks (coracobrachialis/biceps detachment); Phase I ROM: 0-3 wks flexion to 90°, ER to 20°; 4-6 wks flexion to 135°, IR to 50°, ER to 30°; if remplissage: limit eccentric infraspinatus strengthening
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): sling 4-6 wks, no active elbow flexion 0-3 wks, restricted ROM by sub-phase; Phase II (6-12 wks): full AROM, progressive ER, RC strengthening 0-45°; Phase III (12 wks+): <10% LSI strength, ER ≥90° at 90° ABD; Phase IV (16-18 wks): plyometrics, sport biomechanics; Phase V (24+ wks): RTS (overhead: 100% Biodex/ASH/grip; contact: +CKCUET)
Notes: No active elbow flexion for 3 weeks post-op due to coracobrachialis/short head biceps detachment. More restricted initial ROM than Bankart (flexion to 90° only weeks 0-3). Coracoid bone-block procedure for >20% glenoid bone loss. Same RTS criteria as 2025 Bankart version. Copyright 2025.

Surgery Type: Shoulder Debridement, Decompression and Distal Clavicle Excision
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling as needed for comfort; avoid repetitive overhead activities Phase II; no swimming until 5/5 RC at 90° (Phase III); post-rehab soreness should resolve within 12 hours
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 wks): sling as needed, PROM/AAROM all planes except horizontal adduction, isometrics; Phase II (4-5 wks): AROM, RC strengthening 0-45°, neuromuscular control; Phase III (7-8 wks): 5/5 RC at 90°, sport biomechanics; Phase IV (10-12 wks): RTS (surgeon and PT clearance)
Notes: Covers arthroscopic debridement of massive/irreparable RC or labral tears, subacromial decompression (acromioplasty), distal clavicle excision (Mumford procedure), and biceps tenotomy. 4-phase protocol; much shorter than repair protocols (RTS ~10-12 weeks). Updated 5/2019.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling immobilization; no long head biceps tension for 6 weeks (no long lever arm flexion, no resisted supination or elbow flexion); ER to 40° in neutral for first 4 weeks; no ABD with ER for 6 weeks; Dr. Scerpella: no forward flexion >130° for 6 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-4-6 wks): sling, no biceps tension 6 wks, ER to 40° neutral; Phase II (6-12 wks): full ROM, gradual biceps tension from weeks 6-8; Phase III (10 wks+): 5/5 RC at 90°, no overhead sports; Phase IV (16 wks+): high velocity, plyometrics; Phase V (22 wks+): sport-specific, RTS (80-90% return to throwing/contact sports per literature)
Notes: Long head biceps protection is the critical restriction for 6 weeks. Type II SLAP is most common type requiring surgery. Posterior capsule tightness common; monitor and treat throughout. Surgeon-specific note for Dr. Scerpella patients. Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS. Copyright 2011.

Surgery Type: Rotator Cuff Repair (Type I and Type II) and Isolated Subscapularis Repair
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Type I: sling 4-8 weeks, no AROM, no active abduction 8 weeks, no supraspinatus resistance 12 weeks. Type II: sling 6-8 weeks, no AROM, no active abduction 9-10 weeks, no supraspinatus resistance 14 weeks. No lifting/supporting body weight. EMG-guided exercise selection throughout.
Timeline / Phases: Type I — Phase I (0-2 wks): sling, PROM ≤15% EMG; Phase II (2-4 wks): PROM progression; Phase III (8-12 wks): AAROM/AROM initiation; Phase IV (3-5 mo): full ROM, gradual strengthening; Phase V (18-22 wks): high demand/RTS. Type II — same structure but 2-4 weeks more conservative at each phase (AROM delayed, abduction restricted longer).
Notes: Single document covering Type I (smaller/better quality), Type II (larger/more complex), and isolated subscapularis repair. EMG-based exercise selection (≤15%, ≤30%, 30-49%, >50%) at each phase. Sling duration decided jointly by physician and PT based on tear size, tissue quality, and stiffness. No publication date in document.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: Week 1: hard brace at 90°; Week 2: hinged brace 30°-100°; Week 3: 15°-110°; brace discontinued 6-8 weeks; no valgus stress throughout; no running/jumping until Phase IV (75% speed week 16); ice 15-20 min after rehab; RTS competitive throwing 8-12 months
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 wks): immobilized 90°, sub-max isometrics wk2; Phase II (4-8 wks): hinged brace ROM progressions, isotonics, no running/jumping; Phase III (9-12 wks): full ROM by wk10, eccentrics, no running/jumping; Phase IV (13-20 wks): arm >45° ABD, 2-hand plyometrics, throwing mechanics education, running 75% wk16; Phase V (21-36 wks): interval throwing program wk28, sport-specific, RTS 8-12 months
Notes: Tommy John surgery for UCL tears in overhead/throwing athletes. Docking or figure-8 tendon graft technique. Flexor muscles split (not detached). Ulnar nerve may be rerouted anteriorly if irritated. RTS competitive throwing 8-12 months; not all athletes return. Updated 3/2018. Copyright 2018.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: Week 1: hard brace 90°; Week 2: brace 30°-100°, rehab ROM 15°-110°; Week 3: brace 20°-100°; brace off 6-8 weeks; no valgus stress; BFR allowed 4-6 weeks post-op; 80% LSI for interval hitting at 10 wks; 90% LSI for interval throwing at 12 wks; RTS 6-9 months
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 wks): hard brace 90°, isometrics wk2; Phase II (3-8 wks): hinged brace ROM progressions, isotonics wk4, brace off wk7, BFR 4-6 wks, grip/HHD testing wk8; Phase III (8-12 wks): full ROM, plyometrics wk8, interval throwing wk12 (90% LSI); Phase IV (12-16 wks): arm >90° ABD, interval throwing program, sprinting wk16; Phase V (20-24 wks): plyometrics, sport-specific, RTS with grip/HHD/Biodex/ASH pass
Notes: Repair (suture anchor + fiber tape) for avulsed UCL with intact, healthy ligament — not reconstruction. Shorter RTS than UCL reconstruction: 6-9 months vs 8-12 months. 80% LSI for interval hitting (10 wks), 90% LSI for throwing (12 wks). Neurocognitive strategies in Phases IV-V. Copyright 2024.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: Splint at 90° neutral forearm 10-14 days; extension block set by surgeon based on repair tension, progressed 10°/week by PT; avoid shoulder extension Phases II-III; no eccentric biceps until Phase IV; 5 lb active shoulder lifting restriction Phase II; RTS with <15% strength difference and surgeon/PT clearance
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): splint at 90° neutral, no rehab appointments; Phase II (2-4 wks): PROM flexion/supination, AROM extension/pronation per brace limits, sub-max biceps/triceps isometrics, 5 lb shoulder restriction; Phase III (5-12 wks): progressive AROM to full ROM, isotonics at 8 wks, multi-plane at 8 wks; Phase IV (12+ wks): multi-planar high velocity, eccentrics, sport-specific, RTS <15% strength difference
Notes: Distal biceps reattachment to radius via suture anchor. Extension block set individually at first post-op visit; PT progresses 10°/week. No eccentric biceps loading until Phase IV. Return to sport/work requires <15% strength difference and surgeon + PT clearance. Updated 11/2018. Copyright 2018.

Surgery Type: Loose Body Removal and Arthroscopic Debridement of the Elbow
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: 5-pound lifting restriction 1-2 weeks; no gripping or impact Phase I; no effusion before Phase II advancement; avoid elbow pain >4/10 during activity; post-exercise pain lasting >12 hours = overexertion; post-activity soreness resolves within 24 hours
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): AAROM hand/wrist/elbow, shoulder AROM, no gripping/impact, 5 lb limit; Phase II (3-6 wks): full AROM flexion/extension/supination/pronation, low load isotonics biceps/triceps/forearm; Phase III (7-12 wks): reactive strengthening, sport-specific programs (interval throwing/hitting), proximal/core strengthening; RTS with therapist clearance based on strength/endurance/mobility
Notes: Arthroscopic elbow procedure covering loose body removal, cartilage debridement, bone spur removal, and capsular/scar tissue release. Short 3-phase protocol; RTS typically by 12 weeks. Updated 11/2019. Copyright 2019.

Surgery Type: Lateral Epicondyler Debridement and Repair of the Elbow
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine Physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: 5-pound lifting restriction 2 weeks; wrist splint first 4 weeks; no gripping or impact Phase I; avoid elbow pain >4/10 during activity; post-activity soreness resolves within 24 hours; 5/5 flexor/extensor strength before Phase III
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): AAROM hand/wrist/elbow, wrist splint, shoulder AROM, no gripping/impact; Phase II (3-6 wks): flexor/extensor strengthening (sub-max isometrics progressing), wrist splint until 4 wks; Phase III (7-12 wks): eccentrics, progressive muscular endurance, proximal/core strengthening, sport-specific; RTS with therapist clearance
Notes: Surgical debridement of extensor tendon degeneration at lateral epicondyle (tennis elbow/lateral epicondylalgia); larger tears may include tendon repair/suturing. Isometrics emphasized in Phase II for analgesic and strengthening effects on tendinopathy. Updated 11/2019. Copyright 2019.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): UW Health Sports Rehabilitation; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: N/A (elbow surgery)
Key Restrictions: No weight bearing through surgical arm (no pushing/pressing including own body weight) for 12 weeks; no resisted elbow or shoulder extension weeks 0-6; shoulder elevation limited to 90° weeks 0-6; no lifting >5 lbs weeks 7-12; triceps resisted strengthening begins Phase IV (12-18 wks); re-rupture rate <5% with protocol compliance
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): splint, wrist AROM/shoulder to 90°, no WB on arm; Phase II (2-6 wks): hinged brace 0°-60° wks 3-4 → 0°-90° wks 5-6, passive/AAROM elbow, active shoulder to 90°; Phase III (7-12 wks): progressive AROM to full ROM by 12 wks, 5 lb limit, RC isometrics; Phase IV (12-18 wks): resisted triceps (BFR start, concentric → eccentric, wall push-ups), RC >90° ABD, sprinting 75% wk16; Phase V (18-20+ wks): interval throwing, plyometrics, sport-specific, RTS isokinetic testing
Notes: Distal triceps tendon reattachment via bone tunnels or suture anchors. Critical: no pushing/pressing movements including own body weight for 12 weeks (re-rupture rate <5% with compliance). Triceps strengthening delayed until Phase IV. Neurocognitive strategies Phases IV-V. Copyright 2024.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Julie Sherry, DPT, MS; Marc Sherry, DPT, LAT, CSCS; Scott Tauferner, PT, LAT; UW Health Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: Full WB; no sports/impact 4-8 weeks; impact loading Phase 4+
Key Restrictions: Acute: 4-8 weeks complete rest/no sports participation, no active or passive lumbar extension Phases 1-2; brace if still symptomatic after 2-4 weeks rest; reduce backpack weight; avoid sleeping prone; vitamin D and calcium recommended; Chronic: activity reduction then immediate stabilization exercise — no bracing or extended rest
Timeline / Phases: ACUTE (5-phase): Phase 1 (4-8 wks): complete rest, abdominal bracing, stretching, no lumbar extension; Phase 2 (+1-4 wks): static stabilization, bridges, planks, no lumbar extension; Phase 3 (+2-4 wks): dynamic trunk stabilization, single-leg bridges, resume lumbar extension in non-WB; Phase 4 (+2-6 wks): athletic enhancement, impact loading progression (2-foot → bounding → single-leg hop), running; Phase 5: RTS/maintenance program. CHRONIC (2-phase): Phase 1: dynamic stabilization + sport modification; Phase 2: athletic enhancement + impact loading; Phase 3: RTS/maintenance.
Notes: Single document covering both acute/progressive (5-phase) and chronic (2-phase) spondylolysis/spondylolisthesis protocols. Healing rates: ~70% unilateral early, 28% progressive/terminal; 85% good clinical outcomes by year 1 regardless of bone healing. Deep abdominal and multifidus core training is central throughout. Authors: Julie Sherry DPT MS, Marc Sherry DPT LAT CSCS, Scott Tauferner PT LAT. Updated 4/2022.

WB Status: N/A (shoulder surgery)
Key Restrictions: Sling continuously Phase I; sling whenever not doing rehab Phase II; PROM only: ABD starts 30° (+5°/wk), flexion starts 45° (+5°/wk), ER at side starts 5° (+5°/wk, max 30°, no abducted positions); no pec major actions (shoulder IR/adduction/flexion) until Phase III; ER limited to 45° in abducted positions Phase III; no horizontal abduction until Phase IV; bench press capped at 50% pre-injury max weeks 18-22
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): sling, elbow/wrist/neck AROM only; Phase II (2-6 wks): PROM per weekly progression (ABD, flex, ER per precautions), shoulder isometrics except pec actions; Phase III (6-12 wks): sub-max pec isometrics in shortened position only, full ROM by wk12, wean sling; Phase IV (12-18 wks): progressive pec isotonics → eccentrics, RC at 90°, bench 50% max wks 18-22; Phase V (18+ wks): progressive to full range eccentrics/plyometrics, sport-specific, RTS with surgeon clearance and UE testing
Notes: Pectoralis major tendon repair (cortical buttons or suture anchors) for complete rupture. Common in weightlifters and football players. Motion restrictions are opposite of posterior shoulder instability: limit extension, horizontal abduction, and ER early. No horizontal abduction loading (bench press, push-ups) until Phase IV with 50% max cap through weeks 18-22. No publication date. UW Health Sports Medicine group.

Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Ken Krogman, MSPT, ATC; Marc Sherry, PT, DPT, LAT, CSCS; Dr. John Wilson; UW Sports Medicine physician group
WB Status: Varies by body part: upper extremity sling 0-10 days; lower extremity PWB with crutches ± boot 0-10 days; wean to full WB Phase II
Key Restrictions: Phase I (0-3 days): immobilization — sling for RC/biceps, wrist splint for elbow epicondyles, PWB + crutches for patellar/quad tendons, PWB + crutches + boot for Achilles/ankle/plantar fascia; no overstressing tendon through exercise/lifting/impact until Phase III; avoid high velocity/plyometrics/running/jumping/heavy lifting until Phase IV
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-3 days): immobilization per body part, gentle AROM; Phase II (3-10-14 days): wean from device, AROM 3x daily, cross-training opposite extremity; Phase III (14 days to 6-8 wks): full ROM, isometric → concentric → eccentric strengthening, proprioception, non-impact cardio; Phase IV (6-8+ wks): eccentric multi-plane strength, impact control, sport-specific progressions, RTS with physician/PT clearance
Notes: Non-surgical PRP injection for tendinopathy across multiple body regions: rotator cuff, biceps, medial/lateral epicondyle, patellar tendon, quad tendon, Achilles, ankle tendons, plantar fascia. Rehab appointments begin 10-14 days post-injection. Authors: Ken Krogman MSPT ATC, Marc Sherry PT DPT LAT CSCS, Dr. John Wilson. Updated 04/2014. Copyright 2014.

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: Partial weight bearing (50%) for 0-6 weeks; progress to full weight bearing ~5-6 weeks post-op
Key Restrictions: No active hip abduction; no passive hip adduction; avoid extreme IR/ER; passive flexion to ~90 degrees; imperative glute max activates before progressing WB
Timeline / Phases: 0-6 wks: 50% PWB, passive ROM, isometrics (glutes/quads/HS/adductors), well-biking; 5-6 wks: quadruped glute kickback, bridging, progress to FWB; 6-12 wks: hip abduction isometrics, gait training, double-leg squat backs, scar mobilization; 3-6 months: tri-planar CKC gluteal strength, proprioception, plyometrics/running if adequate strength
Notes: PT starts 2-4 weeks post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; revised 2025

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD
WB Status: 50% PWB with crutches for 2 weeks, then wean crutches
Key Restrictions: No ER >neutral; no hip flexion >90 degrees; no early active hip flexion (hip flexor tendonitis risk); no driving until ~4 weeks post-op; naproxen for heterotopic ossification prophylaxis; aspirin for DVT prophylaxis
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-6 wks): 50% PWB, passive ROM, isometrics, stationary bike (high seat); Phase II (6-12 wks): normalize ROM/strength/gait, progressive strengthening/neuromuscular training, elliptical/treadmill; Phase III (3-5 months): running progression, sport-specific drills, plyometrics; Return to sport 5-6 months
Notes: PT starts 2-3 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; sutures out 10-14 days; 2025

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: Full weight bearing immediately; no brace
Key Restrictions: No brace required; criteria-based progression through 5 phases
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (1-6 wks): FWB, quad activation, patellar mob, CKC strengthening (leg press 90-30 deg), stationary bike; Phase II (6-12 wks): stair climbing, CKC progression, elliptical, proprioception; Phase III (3-6 months): running, isokinetics, plyometrics, agility; Phase IV (6 months): return to practice; Phase V (8-9 months): full return to activity at surgeon discretion
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; criteria-based advancement; isokinetic testing at 12 weeks recommended; revised 2025

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: TDWB x 6 weeks for radial/root repairs; WBAT for all other meniscal repair types
Key Restrictions: 0-90 degrees flexion for first 6 weeks; WB status depends on repair type (radial/root vs. other); D/C brace and advance to WBAT at week 6
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (1-6 wks): WB per repair type, 0-90 deg flexion, quad sets, CKC, stationary bike; Phase II (6-12 wks): D/C brace, WBAT, stair climbing, CKC progression, elliptical; Phase III (3-6 months): running, isokinetics, plyometrics, agility; Phase IV (6 months): return to practice; Phase V (8-9 months): full return to activity
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; radial/root repairs have strictest WB restriction; revised 2025

Surgery Type: ACL with MCL or Posterolateral Corner (PLC) Reconstruction
Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: TDWB x 6 weeks with crutches; D/C brace and advance to WBAT at week 6
Key Restrictions: TDWB x 6 weeks; brace full ROM, sleep locked in extension; 0-120 degrees AROM first 4 weeks
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (1-6 wks): TDWB in brace, quad sets/SLR, CKC, stationary bike; Phase II (6-12 wks): D/C brace, WBAT, stair climbing, elliptical, proprioception; Phase III (3-6 months): running, isokinetics, plyometrics, agility (quad/HS 85% symmetry criteria); Phase IV (6 months): return to practice; Phase V (8-9 months): full return to activity
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; covers ACL+MCL or ACL+PLC; revised 2025

Surgery Type: ACL and PCL with Collateral Ligament Reconstruction
Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: Touch down weight bearing (TDWB) x 6 weeks; then full WB as tolerated
Key Restrictions: PCL stabilizing brace 3-6 months; brace locked at 0 degrees for first 2 weeks, full ROM weeks 2-6; all ROM in prone/sidelying for first 6 weeks; no hamstring isometrics; brace worn daytime until 6 months
Timeline / Phases: 0-6 wks: TDWB in brace, quad sets/SLR (no ham isometrics), stationary bike (high seat), leg press bilateral; 6-12 wks: aquatic therapy, wean crutches to FWB, treadmill walk/retro, step-ups, single-leg balance; 3-6 months: hamstring strengthening, jogging assessment, plyometrics (40-100 foot contacts/session), isokinetics; 6 months: D/C brace, running/sport-specific drills, isokinetic test (quad diff <=15%, HS/quad ratio >=65%); return to activity at surgeon discretion
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; most restrictive of UNM knee protocols; revised 2025

Surgery Type: Extensor Mechanism Repair (Patella/Quad Tendon or Patella ORIF)
Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: WBAT with brace locked in extension; progress to FWB as swelling and quad recruitment allow
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension Phase I; ROM and brace progression goals set by surgeon (typically 0-90 deg by ~week 6); brace D/C week surgeon-determined
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): WBAT in brace locked in extension, quad sets/SLR, hip exercises; Phase II (2-6 wks): ROM 0-X deg increasing X/wk per surgeon, heel slides, step ups, CKC TKE, proprioception; Phase III (6-12 wks): D/C brace per surgeon, CKC, advanced quad/HS strengthening, core; Phase IV (3-6 months): running, sport-specific drills, agility
Notes: PT starts 2 weeks post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; covers patella tendon repair, quad tendon repair, or patella ORIF; surgeon completes individual ROM and brace duration goals on protocol page 2; revised 2025

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: WBAT; discontinue crutches as swelling and quad recruitment dictate
Key Restrictions: No specific restrictions; progress through 3 phases as tolerated
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (Acute): WBAT, quad sets, SLR, heel slides, half squats, stationary bike; Phase II (Internal): FWB, cardio equipment (stairmaster/elliptical/treadmill/bike), lunges, leg press, step ups, knee extensions 90-40 deg, proprioception; Phase III (Advanced): plyometrics, running/agility program, sport-specific drills; Discharge: non-antalgic gait, pain-free full ROM, LE strength >=4+/5, resolved edema
Notes: PT starts 7-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; general protocol not specific to a procedure; revised 2025

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: Closing wedge: WBAT (may need crutches 2-6 wks); Opening wedge: 50% PWB x 6 weeks; Both: full WB at 6 weeks
Key Restrictions: Brace unlocked per surgeon-set arc x 6 weeks; no pivoting/twisting/hopping/jumping/running until 6 weeks post-op; leg press max 50% BW x 0-6 weeks
Timeline / Phases: 0-6 wks: WB per type, brace per surgeon, quad sets, SLR, heel slides, aquatic therapy, stationary bike, leg press (50% BW max); 6 wks: FWB, D/C brace, gait normalization, isokinetics, treadmill walk/retro, proprioception; 12 wks: sport-specific drills, plyometrics, quad symmetry <=15%; 4-6 months: full return to sport (pivoting/squatting/twisting/running)
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; WB and brace arc differ by osteotomy type; monitor for patellofemoral symptoms; revised 2025

Surgery Type: Meniscal Repair (Root/Radial or All Other Types)
Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: Root or radial repair: TDWB x 6 weeks; All other repair types: WBAT; Full WB at 6 weeks for all
Key Restrictions: Brace 0-90 degrees x 6 weeks; crutches x 6 weeks; no pivoting/twisting/hopping/jumping/running until 6 weeks post-op; leg press max 50% BW x 0-6 weeks
Timeline / Phases: 0-6 wks: brace 0-90 deg, WB per repair type, quad sets, SLR, aquatic therapy, stationary bike, leg press (50% BW max); 6 wks: FWB, D/C brace, gait normalization, isokinetics, treadmill walk/retro, proprioception; 12 wks: sport-specific drills, plyometrics, quad symmetry <=15%; 4-6 months: full return to sport

Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): WBAT, brace locked in extension if TTO, quad sets/SLR, hip exercises; Phase II (2-6 wks): FWB, brace open 0-90 deg if TTO, step ups, CKC TKE, proprioception; Phase III (6-12 wks): D/C brace if TTO, CKC, advanced quad/HS strengthening, core; Phase IV (3-6 months): running, sport-specific drills, agility; full return at surgeon discretion

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine

WB Status: TDWB x 6 weeks with crutches; full WB as tolerated at 6-12 weeks
Key Restrictions: PCL stabilizing brace 3-6 months; brace locked at 0 degrees first 2 weeks, full ROM weeks 2-6; all ROM in prone/sidelying x 6 weeks; no hamstring isometrics; brace for all daytime until 6 months

Source Organization: University of New Mexico (UNM) Sports Medicine
Surgeon(s) / Author(s): Will Curtis MD, Dustin Richter MD, Christopher Shultz MD, Gehron Treme MD, Andrew Veitch MD
WB Status: WBAT with brace locked in extension; progress to FWB as swelling and quad recruitment allow
Key Restrictions: Brace locked in extension Phase I; ROM 0-45 degrees increasing 15 deg/week to reach 0-90 deg by week 6; brace arc corresponds to ROM progression
Timeline / Phases: Phase I (0-2 wks): WBAT in brace locked in extension, quad sets/SLR; Phase II (2-6 wks): ROM 0-45 deg increasing 15 deg/wk to 90 by week 6, step ups, CKC; Phase III (4-10 wks): stair climbing, CKC progression, elliptical, pool/unweighted treadmill running at 8 weeks; Phase IV (10-16 wks): OKC knee extensions 90-30 deg, isokinetics, full WB running at 12 weeks; Phase V (4-6 months): plyometrics, agility (85% symmetry criteria); Phase VI (6 months+): return to sport with team
Notes: PT starts 3-10 days post-op, 1-2x/week for 12+ weeks; 6-phase criteria-based protocol; distinct from ACL reconstruction; revised 2025
